4.NET:
1. Now, Israel, pay attention to the statutes and ordinances I am about to teach you, so that you might live and go on to enter and take possession of the land that the Lord, the God of your ancestors, is giving you.
2. Do not add a thing to what I command you nor subtract from it, so that you may keep the commandments of the Lord your God that I am delivering to you.
3. You have witnessed what the Lord did at Baal Peor, how he eradicated from your midst everyone who followed Baal Peor.
4. But you who remained faithful to the Lord your God are still alive to this very day, every one of you.
5. Look! I have taught you statutes and ordinances just as the Lord my God told me to do, so that you might carry them out in the land you are about to enter and possess.
6. So be sure to do them, because this will testify of your wise understanding to the people who will learn of all these statutes and say, “Indeed, this great nation is a very wise people.”
7. In fact, what other great nation has a god so near to them like the Lord our God whenever we call on him?
8. And what other great nation has statutes and ordinances as just as this whole law that I am about to share with you today?
9. Again, however, pay very careful attention, lest you forget the things you have seen and disregard them for the rest of your life; instead teach them to your children and grandchildren.
10. You stood before the Lord your God at Horeb and he said to me, “Assemble the people before me so that I can tell them my commands. Then they will learn to revere me all the days they live in the land, and they will instruct their children.”
11. You approached and stood at the foot of the mountain, a mountain ablaze to the sky above it and yet dark with a thick cloud.
12. Then the Lord spoke to you from the middle of the fire; you heard speech but you could not see anything—only a voice was heard.
13. And he revealed to you the covenant he has commanded you to keep, the Ten Commandments, writing them on two stone tablets.
14. Moreover, at that same time the Lord commanded me to teach you statutes and ordinances for you to keep in the land that you are about to enter and possess.
15. Be very careful, then, because you saw no form at the time the Lord spoke to you at Horeb from the middle of the fire.
16. I say this so you will not corrupt yourselves by making an image in the form of any kind of figure. This includes the likeness of a human male or female,
17. any kind of land animal, any bird that flies in the sky,
18. anything that crawls on the ground, or any fish in the deep waters under the earth.
19. When you look up to the sky and see the sun, moon, and stars—the whole heavenly creation—you must not be seduced to worship and serve them, for the Lord your God has assigned them to all the people of the world.
20. You, however, the Lord has selected and brought from Egypt, that iron-smelting furnace, to be his special people as you are today.
21. But the Lord became angry with me because of you and vowed that I would never cross the Jordan nor enter the good land that he is about to give you.
22. So I must die here in this land; I will not cross the Jordan. But you are going over and will possess that good land.
23. Be on guard so that you do not forget the covenant of the Lord your God that he has made with you, and that you do not make an image of any kind, just as he has forbidden you.
24. For the Lord your God is a consuming fire; he is a jealous God.
25. After you have produced children and grandchildren and have been in the land a long time, if you become corrupt and make an image of any kind and do other evil things before the Lord your God that enrage him,
26. I invoke heaven and earth as witnesses against you today that you will surely and swiftly be removed from the very land you are about to cross the Jordan to possess. You will not last long there because you will surely be annihilated.
27. Then the Lord will scatter you among the peoples and there will be very few of you among the nations where the Lord will drive you.
28. There you will worship gods made by human hands—wood and stone that can neither see, hear, eat, nor smell.
29. But if you seek the Lord your God from there, you will find him, if, indeed, you seek him with all your heart and soul.
30. In your distress when all these things happen to you in future days, if you return to the Lord your God and obey him
31. (for he is a merciful God), he will not let you down or destroy you, for he cannot forget the covenant with your ancestors that he confirmed by oath to them.
32. Indeed, ask about the distant past, starting from the day God created humankind on the earth, and ask from one end of heaven to the other, whether there has ever been such a great thing as this, or even a rumor of it.
33. Have a people ever heard the voice of God speaking from the middle of fire, as you yourselves have, and lived to tell about it?
34. Or has God ever before tried to deliver a nation from the middle of another nation, accompanied by judgments, signs, wonders, war, strength, power, and other very terrifying things like the Lord your God did for you in Egypt before your very eyes?
35. You have been taught that the Lord alone is God—there is no other besides him.
36. From heaven he spoke to you in order to teach you, and on earth he showed you his great fire from which you also heard his words.
37. Moreover, because he loved your ancestors, he chose their descendants who followed them and personally brought you out of Egypt with his great power
38. to dispossess nations greater and stronger than you and brought you here this day to give you their land as your property.
39. Today realize and carefully consider that the Lord is God in heaven above and on earth below—there is no other!
40. Keep his statutes and commandments that I am setting forth today so that it may go well with you and your descendants and that you may enjoy longevity in the land that the Lord your God is about to give you as a permanent possession.
41. Then Moses selected three cities in the Transjordan, toward the east.
42. Anyone who accidentally killed someone without hating him at the time of the accident could flee to one of those cities and be safe.
43. These cities are Bezer, in the wilderness plateau, for the Reubenites; Ramoth in Gilead for the Gadites; and Golan in Bashan for the Manassehites.
44. This is the law that Moses set before the Israelites.
45. These are the stipulations, statutes, and ordinances that Moses spoke to the Israelites after he had brought them out of Egypt,
46. in the Transjordan, in the valley opposite Beth Peor, in the land of King Sihon of the Amorites, who lived in Heshbon. (It is he whom Moses and the Israelites attacked after they came out of Egypt.
47. They possessed his land and that of King Og of Bashan—both of whom were Amorite kings in the Transjordan, to the east.
48. Their territory extended from Aroer at the edge of the Arnon valley as far as Mount Siyon—that is, Hermon—
49. including all the rift valley of the Transjordan in the east to the sea of the rift valley, beneath the slopes of Pisgah.)
4.WEB:
1. Now, Israel, listen to the statutes and to the ordinances, which I teach you, to do them; that you may live, and go in and possess the land which Yahweh, the God of your fathers, gives you.
2. You shall not add to the word which I command you, neither shall you take away from it, that you may keep the commandments of Yahweh your God which I command you.
3. Your eyes have seen what Yahweh did because of Baal Peor; for all the men who followed Baal Peor, Yahweh your God has destroyed them from among you.
4. But you who were faithful to Yahweh your God are all alive today.
5. Behold, I have taught you statutes and ordinances, even as Yahweh my God commanded me, that you should do so in the middle of the land where you go in to possess it.
6. Keep therefore and do them; for this is your wisdom and your understanding in the sight of the peoples who shall hear all these statutes and say, “Surely this great nation is a wise and understanding people.”
7. For what great nation is there, that has a god so near to them, as Yahweh our God is whenever we call on him?
8. What great nation is there, that has statutes and ordinances so righteous as all this law, which I set before you today?
9. Only be careful, and keep your soul diligently, lest you forget the things which your eyes saw, and lest they depart from your heart all the days of your life; but make them known to your children and your children’s children;
10. the day that you stood before Yahweh your God in Horeb, when Yahweh said to me, “Assemble the people to me, and I will make them hear my words, that they may learn to fear me all the days that they live on the earth, and that they may teach their children.”
11. You came near and stood under the mountain. The mountain burned with fire to the heart of the sky, with darkness, cloud, and thick darkness.
12. Yahweh spoke to you out of the middle of the fire: you heard the voice of words, but you saw no form; you only heard a voice.
13. He declared to you his covenant, which he commanded you to perform, even the ten commandments. He wrote them on two stone tablets.
14. Yahweh commanded me at that time to teach you statutes and ordinances, that you might do them in the land where you go over to possess it.
15. Be very careful, for you saw no kind of form on the day that Yahweh spoke to you in Horeb out of the middle of the fire,
16. lest you corrupt yourselves, and make yourself a carved image in the form of any figure, the likeness of male or female,
17. the likeness of any animal that is on the earth, the likeness of any winged bird that flies in the sky,
18. the likeness of anything that creeps on the ground, the likeness of any fish that is in the water under the earth;
19. and lest you lift up your eyes to the sky, and when you see the sun and the moon and the stars, even all the army of the sky, you are drawn away and worship them, and serve them, which Yahweh your God has allotted to all the peoples under the whole sky.
20. But Yahweh has taken you, and brought you out of the iron furnace, out of Egypt, to be to him a people of inheritance, as it is today.
21. Furthermore Yahweh was angry with me for your sakes, and swore that I should not go over the Jordan, and that I should not go in to that good land, which Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance;
22. but I must die in this land. I must not go over the Jordan; but you shall go over, and possess that good land.
23. Be careful, lest you forget the covenant of Yahweh your God, which he made with you, and make yourselves a carved image in the form of anything which Yahweh your God has forbidden you.
24. For Yahweh your God is a devouring fire, a jealous God.
25. When you shall father children, and children’s children, and you shall have been long in the land, and shall corrupt yourselves, and make a carved image in the form of anything, and shall do that which is evil in Yahweh your God’s sight, to provoke him to anger;
26. I call heaven and earth to witness against you today, that you will soon utterly perish from off the land which you go over the Jordan to possess it. You will not prolong your days on it, but will utterly be destroyed.
27. Yahweh will scatter you among the peoples, and you will be left few in number among the nations, where Yahweh will lead you away.
28. There you shall serve gods, the work of men’s hands, wood and stone, which neither see, nor hear, nor eat, nor smell.
29. But from there you shall seek Yahweh your God, and you shall find him, when you search after him with all your heart and with all your soul.
30. When you are in oppression, and all these things have come on you, in the latter days you shall return to Yahweh your God, and listen to his voice.
31. For Yahweh your God is a merciful God. He will not fail you, neither destroy you, nor forget the covenant of your fathers which he swore to them.
32. For ask now of the days that are past, which were before you, since the day that God created man on the earth, and from the one end of the sky to the other, whether there has been anything as this great thing is, or has been heard like it?
33. Did a people ever hear the voice of God speaking out of the middle of the fire, as you have heard, and live?
34. Or has God tried to go and take a nation for himself from among another nation, by trials, by signs, by wonders, by war, by a mighty hand, by an outstretched arm, and by great terrors, according to all that Yahweh your God did for you in Egypt before your eyes?
35. It was shown to you so that you might know that Yahweh is God. There is no one else besides him.
36. Out of heaven he made you to hear his voice, that he might instruct you. On earth he made you to see his great fire; and you heard his words out of the middle of the fire.
37. Because he loved your fathers, therefore he chose their offspring after them, and brought you out with his presence, with his great power, out of Egypt;
38. to drive out nations from before you greater and mightier than you, to bring you in, to give you their land for an inheritance, as it is today.
39. Know therefore today, and take it to heart, that Yahweh himself is God in heaven above and on the earth beneath. There is no one else.
40. You shall keep his statutes, and his commandments, which I command you today, that it may go well with you, and with your children after you, and that you may prolong your days in the land, which Yahweh your God gives you, forever.
41. Then Moses set apart three cities beyond the Jordan toward the sunrise;
42. that the man slayer might flee there, who kills his neighbor unawares, and didn’t hate him in time past; and that fleeing to one of these cities he might live:
43. Bezer in the wilderness, in the plain country, for the Reubenites; and Ramoth in Gilead, for the Gadites; and Golan in Bashan, for the Manassites.
44. This is the law which Moses set before the children of Israel.
45. These are the testimonies, and the statutes, and the ordinances, which Moses spoke to the children of Israel, when they came out of Egypt,
46. beyond the Jordan, in the valley over against Beth Peor, in the land of Sihon king of the Amorites, who lived at Heshbon, whom Moses and the children of Israel struck, when they came out of Egypt.
47. They took his land in possession, and the land of Og king of Bashan, the two kings of the Amorites, who were beyond the Jordan toward the sunrise;
48. from Aroer, which is on the edge of the valley of the Arnon, even to Mount Sion (also called Hermon),
49. and all the Arabah beyond the Jordan eastward, even to the sea of the Arabah, under the slopes of Pisgah.
4.notes:
Deuteronomy - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Moses urges Israel to obey God's commands, warning against idolatry and reminding them that no nation has a God so near as theirs.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Deuteronomy is the fifth and final book of the Pentateuch,
written at the end of the 40 years of wilderness wandering (approximately
1406 BC). Hebrew title: "Devarim" — "Words/Things." Greek title:
"Deuteronomion" — "Second Law" (a repetition/expansion of the Law for the
new generation). The book consists of Moses' farewell speeches to Israel on
the plains of Moab, just before they enter the Promised Land. Moses will
not enter with them — these are his final words to the nation he has led
for 40 years.

Historical Context: Chapter 4 is the climax of Moses' first address — a
passionate exhortation to obedience. Having reviewed the history (chapters
1-3), Moses now draws the application: remember what you saw, keep the
commandments, do not make idols, and teach your children. This chapter
contains some of the most exalted theology in the Old Testament: God's
uniqueness ("there is none else beside him," v.35), His nearness ("what
nation is there so great, who hath God so nigh unto them?" v.7), His
character as consuming fire and jealous God (v.24), yet also merciful —
He will not forsake His covenant (v.31). Moses warns prophetically of
future apostasy and exile (vv.25-28) but promises restoration for those
who seek God with all their heart (vv.29-31). The chapter concludes with
Moses designating three cities of refuge east of the Jordan (vv.41-43) and
a transitional summary (vv.44-49) that introduces the second address
(chapters 5-26).

The Call to Obedience (vv.1-4): "Hearken, O Israel" (v.1) — listen and do,
"that ye may live, and go in and possess the land." Do not add to or
diminish from God's word (v.2). The warning of Baal-peor is fresh: those
who followed that idol were destroyed; those who "did cleave unto the LORD"
are alive today (vv.3-4). Obedience is life; disobedience is death.

Israel's Unique Wisdom (vv.5-8): Keeping God's statutes is Israel's
"wisdom and understanding in the sight of the nations" (v.6). Two
rhetorical questions: (1) "What nation is there so great, who hath God so
nigh unto them?" (v.7) — no nation has a God who is near and who answers
prayer. (2) "What nation is there so great, that hath statutes and
judgments so righteous?" (v.8) — no nation has laws as just as God's. The
nations will look at Israel and say: "Surely this great nation is a wise
and understanding people" (v.6).

Remember Horeb — No Idols (vv.9-24): Moses commands: "take heed to
thyself, and keep thy soul diligently, lest thou forget" (v.9). Teach your
children and grandchildren (v.9). Remember the day at Horeb: the mountain
burned with fire, darkness, clouds (v.11). God spoke from the fire — "ye
heard the voice of the words, but saw no similitude; only ye heard a voice"
(v.12). This is the theological foundation for the prohibition of images:
God revealed Himself in word, not in form. Therefore: no carved image of
any figure — male, female, animal, bird, creeping thing, fish (vv.16-18).
No worship of sun, moon, or stars (v.19). God took Israel from "the iron
furnace" of Egypt to be His own people (v.20). Warning: "the LORD thy God
is a consuming fire, even a jealous God" (v.24).

Prophetic Warning of Exile and Restoration (vv.25-31): Moses foresees the
future: when they grow old in the land and corrupt themselves with idols
(v.25), they will "utterly perish from off the land" (v.26). God will
scatter them among the nations (v.27). There they will serve gods of wood
and stone — lifeless, senseless (v.28). But — "if from thence thou shalt
seek the LORD thy God, thou shalt find him, if thou seek him with all thy
heart and with all thy soul" (v.29). In tribulation, "in the latter days,"
if they return to God, He will hear (v.30). The reason: "the LORD thy God
is a merciful God; he will not forsake thee, neither destroy thee, nor
forget the covenant of thy fathers" (v.31).

The Uniqueness of God (vv.32-40): Moses appeals to all of history: has
anything like this ever happened? (v.32). Has any people heard God's voice
from fire and lived? (v.33). Has any god taken a nation from another nation
by signs, wonders, war, and mighty hand? (v.34). The conclusion: "the LORD
he is God; there is none else beside him" (v.35). He loved their fathers,
chose their offspring, brought them out of Egypt by His own presence (v.37).
Therefore: "know therefore this day, and consider it in thine heart, that
the LORD he is God in heaven above, and upon the earth beneath: there is
none else" (v.39). Keep His commandments "that it may go well with thee,
and with thy children after thee" (v.40).

Cities of Refuge East of Jordan (vv.41-43): Moses sets apart three cities
for the manslayer who kills unintentionally: Bezer (for Reuben), Ramoth in
Gilead (for Gad), Golan in Bashan (for Manasseh) (v.43). Even in the midst
of theological exhortation, Moses attends to practical justice.

Transitional Summary (vv.44-49): A heading for the second address: "this
is the law which Moses set before the children of Israel" (v.44). Location:
beyond the Jordan, opposite Beth-peor, in the land of Sihon (vv.46-47).
Territory: from Aroer to Hermon, the entire eastern Arabah (vv.48-49).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Moses speaks from the plains of Moab. He warns against idolatry that would lead to exile "among the nations" (v.27).
- Cities of refuge east of Jordan named (vv.41-43): Bezer (Reuben), Ramoth in Gilead (Gad), Golan in Bashan (Manasseh).

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Revelation  | 22      | 18-19              | Do not add to or take from God's word.
v.2              | Proverbs    | 30      | 5-6                | "Do not add to his words."
v.3              | Numbers     | 25      | 1-9                | The Baal-peor incident.
v.7              | Psalm       | 145     | 18                 | "The LORD is near to all who call on him."
v.7              | James       | 4       | 8                  | "Draw near to God, and he will draw near to you."
v.10             | Exodus      | 19      | 9                  | 20; 20:18-21 — The theophany at Horeb/Sinai.
v.12             | Exodus      | 33      | 20                 | ; John 1:18 — No one has seen God's form.
v.13             | Exodus      | 34      | 28                 | The Ten Commandments on two tablets.
v.20             | 1 Kings     | 8       | 51                 | ; Jeremiah 11:4 — Egypt as "iron furnace."
v.24             | Hebrews     | 12      | 29                 | "Our God is a consuming fire."
v.26             | Deuteronomy | 30      | 19                 | Heaven and earth as witnesses.
v.27             | Leviticus   | 26      | 33                 | ; Deuteronomy 28:64 — Scattering among nations.
v.29             | Jeremiah    | 29      | 13                 | "You will seek me and find me, when you seek me with all your heart."
v.31             | Lamentations | 3       | 22-23              | God's mercies are new every morning.
v.34             |             |         |                    | Exodus 7-12 — The signs and wonders in Egypt.
v.35             | Isaiah      | 45      | 5-6                | "I am the LORD, and there is no other."
v.37             | Deuteronomy | 7       | 7-8                | God chose Israel because of love.
v.41             | Numbers     | 35      | 9                  | 34; Joshua 20:1-9 — Cities of refuge.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-4/
  Guzik emphasizes the uniqueness of Israel's relationship with God —
  no other nation had a god who was near, who spoke, who answered prayer.
  He notes that the prohibition of images flows from the nature of the
  Horeb revelation: God spoke but showed no form. He highlights the
  prophetic accuracy of vv.25-31 — fulfilled in the Babylonian exile and
  subsequent restorations.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "What nation is there so great? The greatness of a people is not
  measured by their armies or their wealth but by the nearness of their
  God. Israel's glory was not in chariots but in the presence of the
  Almighty. And so it is with the church: our greatness is not in our
  programs or our numbers but in the nearness of Christ. 'Lo, I am with
  you always' — that is our glory."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "statutes and judgments" / ESV: "statutes and rules" — Hebrew "chuqqim u-mishpatim." "Chuqqim" = statutes/decrees (inscribed laws); "mishpatim" = judgments/rulings (case law). KJV's "judgments" is more literal; ESV's "rules" is more accessible.
v.2              | KJV         | "neither shall ye diminish ought from it" / ESV: "nor take from it" — Hebrew "v'lo tigr'u mimmennu." "Gara" = to diminish, to subtract. The word of God is complete — nothing to add, nothing to remove.
v.6              | KJV         | "this is your wisdom and your understanding" / ESV: "that will be your wisdom and your understanding" — Hebrew "hi chochmat'chem u-vinat'chem." Obedience to God's law IS wisdom — not merely leads to it.
v.11             | KJV         | "the mountain burned with fire unto the midst of heaven" / ESV: "the mountain burned with fire to the heart of heaven" — Hebrew "ad lev ha-shamayim." "Lev" = heart/midst. The fire reached to the very heart of the sky — a terrifying theophany.
v.12             | KJV         | "saw no similitude" / ESV: "saw no form" — Hebrew "t'munah." This word means form, likeness, representation. God had no visible shape — only voice. This is the theological basis for the second commandment.
v.19             | KJV         | "which the LORD thy God hath divided unto all nations" / ESV: "things that the LORD your God has allotted to all the peoples" — Hebrew "asher chalaq YHWH." God "allotted" the heavenly bodies to the nations — not for worship but for light and seasons. The nations corrupted this by worshiping the creation rather than the Creator.
v.20             | KJV         | "the iron furnace" / ESV: "the iron furnace" — Hebrew "kur ha-barzel." A smelting furnace for iron — the hottest, most intense furnace. Egypt was a place of extreme suffering that refined Israel as metal is refined by fire.
v.24             | KJV         | "a consuming fire, even a jealous God" / ESV: "a consuming fire, a jealous God" — Hebrew "esh okhelah... El qanna." "Okhelah" = devouring/consuming. "Qanna" = jealous/zealous. God's jealousy is not insecurity but the righteous demand of a husband for exclusive devotion from His bride.
v.29             | KJV         | "if thou seek him with all thy heart and with all thy soul" / ESV: "if you search after him with all your heart and with all your soul" — Hebrew "b'chol l'vav'cha u-v'chol nafsh'cha." Wholehearted seeking — not casual inquiry but desperate pursuit.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Similitude/Form   | Hebrew "t'munah" — a visible form, likeness, or representation. God revealed Himself at Horeb without visible form — only voice. This makes image-worship not merely forbidden but theologically absurd: you cannot represent what has no form. | 
Consuming Fire    | Hebrew "esh okhelah" — fire that devours. A metaphor for God's holiness that destroys whatever is unholy. Not arbitrary destruction but the natural result of sin encountering absolute purity. | 
Jealous God       | Hebrew "El qanna" — God who is zealous for exclusive relationship. Not petty jealousy but covenantal passion. As a husband is rightly jealous for his wife's fidelity, God demands exclusive worship from His covenant people. | 
Iron Furnace      | Hebrew "kur ha-barzel" — a metaphor for Egypt as a place of intense suffering that refined Israel. The hottest furnace, used for smelting iron. Suffering as purification. | 
Cities of Refuge  | Three cities (later six total) designated as safe havens for those who kill accidentally. The manslayer could flee there and be protected from the avenger of blood until trial. A provision of mercy within justice. | 
Baal              | peor      — The idol worshiped at Peor (Numbers 25). Israel's participation in its worship brought a plague killing 24,000. Moses cites it as a fresh warning: those who followed Baal-peor are dead; those who held fast to God are alive. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "What nation is there so great, who hath God so nigh unto them?" (v.7).
   The greatest privilege is not wealth, power, or wisdom — it is the
   nearness of God. Do you value His presence above all other blessings?

2. "Ye heard the voice... but saw no similitude" (v.12). God is known
   through His word, not through images. Every attempt to reduce God to a
   visible form diminishes Him. He is beyond representation — He can only
   be heard, believed, and obeyed.

3. "Lest thou forget" (v.9). The greatest spiritual danger is not dramatic
   apostasy but quiet forgetting. Memory must be actively maintained —
   through teaching, through repetition, through passing truth to the next
   generation.

4. "A consuming fire, even a jealous God" (v.24). God's love is not passive
   or indifferent. He burns with holy passion for His people's exclusive
   devotion. His jealousy is the measure of His love.

5. "If from thence thou shalt seek the LORD thy God, thou shalt find him"
   (v.29). Even from the depths of exile and judgment, God can be found. No
   distance is too far, no sin too great — if the seeking is wholehearted.
   This is the gospel in seed form.

6. "He will not forsake thee... nor forget the covenant" (v.31). God's
   mercy outlasts His judgment. His covenant is stronger than Israel's
   unfaithfulness. He remembers His promises even when His people forget
   their obligations.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Deuteronomy Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/deuteronomy/

David Guzik — Deuteronomy 4 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-4/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

5.ESV:
1. And Moses summoned all Israel and said to them, “Hear, O Israel, the statutes and the rules that I speak in your hearing today, and you shall learn them and be careful to do them.
2. The LORD our God made a covenant with us in Horeb.
3. Not with our fathers did the LORD make this covenant, but with us, who are all of us here alive today.
4. The LORD spoke with you face to face at the mountain, out of the midst of the fire,
5. while I stood between the LORD and you at that time, to declare to you the word of the LORD. For you were afraid because of the fire, and you did not go up into the mountain. He said:
6. “‘I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.
7. “‘You shall have no other gods before me.
8. “‘You shall not make for yourself a carved image, or any likeness of anything that is in heaven above, or that is on the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth.
9. You shall not bow down to them or serve them, for I the LORD your God am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me,
10. but showing steadfast love to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments.
11. “‘You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain, for the LORD will not hold him guiltless who takes his name in vain.
12. “‘Observe the Sabbath day, to keep it holy, as the LORD your God commanded you.
13. Six days you shall labor and do all your work,
14. but the seventh day is a Sabbath to the LORD your God. On it you shall not do any work, you or your son or your daughter or your male servant or your female servant, or your ox or your donkey or any of your livestock, or the sojourner who is within your gates, that your male servant and your female servant may rest as well as you.
15. You shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt, and the LORD your God brought you out from there with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm. Therefore the LORD your God commanded you to keep the Sabbath day.
16. “‘Honor your father and your mother, as the LORD your God commanded you, that your days may be long, and that it may go well with you in the land that the LORD your God is giving you.
17. “‘You shall not murder.
18. “‘And you shall not commit adultery.
19. “‘And you shall not steal.
20. “‘And you shall not bear false witness against your neighbor.
21. “‘And you shall not covet your neighbor’s wife. And you shall not desire your neighbor’s house, his field, or his male servant, or his female servant, his ox, or his donkey, or anything that is your neighbor’s.’
22. “These words the LORD spoke to all your assembly at the mountain out of the midst of the fire, the cloud, and the thick darkness, with a loud voice; and he added no more. And he wrote them on two tablets of stone and gave them to me.
23. And as soon as you heard the voice out of the midst of the darkness, while the mountain was burning with fire, you came near to me, all the heads of your tribes, and your elders.

14. but the seventh day is the Sabbath of the Lord your God. On that day you must not do any work, you, your son, your daughter, your male slave, your female slave, your ox, your donkey, any other animal, or the resident foreigner who lives with you, so that your male and female slaves, like yourself, may have rest.

26. Who is there from the entire human race who has heard the voice of the living God speaking from the middle of the fire as we have, and has lived?

33. Walk just as he has commanded you so that you may live, that it may go well with you, and that you may live long in the land you are going to possess.

5.notes:
Deuteronomy - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Moses restates the Ten Commandments to the new generation, reminding them of the covenant at Horeb and their duty to obey.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Deuteronomy is the fifth and final book of the Pentateuch,
written at the end of the 40 years of wilderness wandering (approximately
1406 BC). Hebrew title: "Devarim" — "Words/Things." Greek title:
"Deuteronomion" — "Second Law" (a repetition/expansion of the Law for the
new generation). The book consists of Moses' farewell speeches to Israel on
the plains of Moab, just before they enter the Promised Land. Moses will
not enter with them — these are his final words to the nation he has led
for 40 years.

Historical Context: Chapter 5 begins Moses' second address (chapters 5-26),
which forms the heart of Deuteronomy. He opens by restating the Ten
Commandments — the foundation of the covenant — for the new generation.
Most of these people were children or not yet born when God spoke at Horeb.
Yet Moses insists: "Not with our fathers did the LORD make this covenant,
but with us, who are all of us here alive today" (v.3). The covenant is not
merely historical — it is present and binding on each generation. The
Deuteronomy version of the Ten Commandments is nearly identical to Exodus
20, with one significant difference: the Sabbath commandment. In Exodus,
the reason is creation ("for in six days the LORD made heaven and earth");
here, the reason is redemption ("remember that thou wast a servant in the
land of Egypt"). Same command, different motivation — reflecting
Deuteronomy's emphasis on God's saving acts. The chapter concludes with the
people's fear at Horeb and God's poignant response: "O that there were such
an heart in them, that they would fear me, and keep all my commandments
always!" (v.29) — a divine longing that anticipates the new covenant
(Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26).

The Covenant at Horeb (vv.1-5): Moses summons all Israel: "Hear, O Israel"
(v.1) — learn, keep, and do. The covenant was made at Horeb — "not with
our fathers... but with us" (v.3). This is remarkable: the previous
generation is dead, yet Moses says the covenant was made with those standing
before him. The covenant transcends generations. God spoke "face to face"
from the fire (v.4). Moses stood as mediator between God and the people
because they feared the fire (v.5).

The Ten Commandments Restated (vv.6-21):
  First: "I am the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of
  Egypt" (v.6) — God's identity and saving act as the basis for all
  commands.
  Second: No other gods before Me (v.7).
  Third: No graven images (vv.8-10) — God is jealous, visiting iniquity
  to the third and fourth generation of those who hate Him, but showing
  mercy to thousands who love Him.
  Fourth: Do not take God's name in vain (v.11).
  Fifth: Keep the Sabbath holy (vv.12-15) — six days of labor, seventh
  day of rest for all, including servants and animals. The reason here:
  "remember that thou wast a servant in the land of Egypt" (v.15). You
  were slaves — now give rest to all, even your servants.
  Sixth: Honor father and mother — "that thy days may be prolonged, and
  that it may go well with thee" (v.16). The first commandment with
  promise.
  Seventh: Do not kill (v.17).
  Eighth: Do not commit adultery (v.18).
  Ninth: Do not steal (v.19).
  Tenth: Do not bear false witness (v.20).
  Eleventh: Do not covet — neighbor's wife, house, field, servants,
  animals, or anything (v.21).

God Spoke and Added No More (v.22): These words God spoke to the whole
assembly from fire, cloud, and thick darkness "with a great voice: and he
added no more" (KJV) / "with a loud voice; and he added no more" (ESV).
The Ten Commandments are complete — God Himself drew the boundary. He wrote
them on two tablets of stone and gave them to Moses.

The People's Fear and Request (vv.23-27): Hearing God's voice from the
fire, the people were terrified (v.23). They acknowledged: "we have seen
this day that God doth talk with man, and he liveth" (v.24). But they
feared death: "if we hear the voice of the LORD our God any more, then we
shall die" (v.25). Their request: let Moses be the mediator — "Go thou
near, and hear all... and speak thou unto us" (v.27). They promise: "we
will hear it, and do it."

God's Response — The Longing Heart (vv.28-33): God heard and approved their
words: "they have well said all that they have spoken" (v.28). Then the
most poignant verse: "O that there were such an heart in them, that they
would fear me, and keep all my commandments always, that it might be well
with them, and with their children for ever!" (v.29). God longs for what He
knows they cannot sustain. Their words are right; their hearts are weak.
This divine longing points forward to the new covenant — when God will
write His law on their hearts (Jeremiah 31:33). Moses is told to remain
and receive the full law to teach them (v.31). The charge: do not turn
aside to the right or left (v.32); walk in all God's ways "that ye may
live" (v.33).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Moses speaks from the plains of Moab. He recounts the giving of the Ten Commandments at Horeb (Sinai) — "The LORD talked with you face to face in the mount out of the midst of the fire" (v.4).

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3              | Hebrews     | 8       | 8-13               | The new covenant that supersedes the old.
v.4              | Exodus      | 19      | 18                 | 20; 20:1 — God speaking face to face at Sinai.
v.6              | Exodus      | 20      | 2                  | The identical preamble in the Exodus version.
v.7              | Exodus      | 20      | 3                  | "You shall have no other gods before me."
v.9              | Exodus      | 34      | 6-7                | God visiting iniquity but showing mercy.
v.12             | Exodus      | 20      | 8-11               | The Sabbath command with creation rationale.
v.15             | Exodus      | 20      | 11                 | Compare: Exodus grounds Sabbath in creation; Deuteronomy grounds it in redemption.
v.16             | Ephesians   | 6       | 2-3                | Paul quotes this as "the first commandment with promise."
v.17             | Matthew     | 5       | 21-22              | Jesus deepens "You shall not murder" to include anger.
v.18             | Matthew     | 5       | 27-28              | Jesus deepens adultery to include lust.
v.21             | Romans      | 7       | 7                  | Paul: "I would not have known sin... 'You shall not covet.'"
v.22             | Exodus      | 31      | 18                 | ; 32:15-16 — The tablets written by God's finger.
v.27             | Hebrews     | 12      | 18-21              | The terror of Sinai contrasted with grace.
v.29             | Jeremiah    | 31      | 31-34              | The new covenant: law written on hearts.
v.29             | Ezekiel     | 36      | 26-27              | "A new heart... and a new spirit."
v.32             | Joshua      | 1       | 7                  | "Do not turn from it to the right hand or to the left."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-5/
  Guzik emphasizes the difference between the Exodus and Deuteronomy
  Sabbath rationales — creation vs. redemption — showing that the same
  command can have multiple valid motivations. He highlights v.29 as one
  of the most moving verses in Scripture: God's own longing for obedient
  hearts, which He knows the old covenant cannot produce.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'O that there were such an heart in them!' Here is the sigh of God
  over His people. He does not merely command obedience — He longs for it.
  He does not merely punish disobedience — He grieves over it. What a God
  is this, who yearns for the love of His creatures! And what a gospel is
  this, that what God longed for in Deuteronomy 5, He provides in the new
  covenant: 'I will put my law in their inward parts, and write it in
  their hearts' (Jeremiah 31:33)."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "statutes and judgments" / ESV: "statutes and rules" — Hebrew "ha-chuqqim v'ha-mishpatim." Same distinction as 4:1. KJV consistently renders "mishpatim" as "judgments"; ESV as "rules."
v.5              | KJV         | "to shew you the word of the LORD" / ESV: "to declare to you the word of the LORD" — Hebrew "l'haggid lachem et d'var YHWH." "Haggid" = to declare, to tell, to make known. Moses' role as mediator: receiving God's word and declaring it to the people.
v.9              | KJV         | "visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children" / ESV: "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children" — Hebrew "poqed avon avot al banim." "Poqed" = visiting/attending to. Not arbitrary punishment of innocents but the natural consequences of sin extending through generations.
v.10             | KJV         | "shewing mercy unto thousands" / ESV: "showing steadfast love to thousands" — Hebrew "oseh chesed la-alaphim." "Chesed" = covenant love/loyalty/mercy. ESV's "steadfast love" captures the covenantal faithfulness; KJV's "mercy" captures the undeserved nature. The contrast: iniquity visits 3-4 generations; mercy extends to thousands.
v.12             | KJV         | "Keep the sabbath day to sanctify it" / ESV: "Observe the Sabbath day, to keep it holy" — Hebrew "shamor et yom ha-Shabbat l'qadd'sho." Note: Exodus 20:8 uses "zachor" (remember); here "shamor" (keep/guard). Jewish tradition holds both were spoken simultaneously — remember and keep.
v.21             | KJV         | "Neither shalt thou desire... neither shalt thou covet" / ESV: "you shall not covet... you shall not desire" — Hebrew "lo tachmod... v'lo tit'avveh." Two different verbs: "chamad" (to desire/delight in) and "avah" (to crave/long for). The Exodus version uses only "chamad." Deuteronomy adds "avah" and separates the wife from the property — elevating her status.
v.29             | KJV         | "O that there were such an heart in them" / ESV: "Oh that they had such a heart as this always" — Hebrew "mi yitten v'hayah l'vavam zeh lahem." Literally "who will give that their heart would be this to them." An idiom expressing deep longing. God Himself wishes for what He knows they lack.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Covenant (Hebrew: "b'rit") | A binding agreement between God and His people. At Horeb, God initiated the covenant with Israel: He would be their God; they would be His people, keeping His commandments. The Ten Commandments are the covenant terms. | 
Ten Commandments  | Hebrew "aseret ha-d'varim" — literally "ten words." The foundational terms of God's covenant with Israel, spoken directly by God and written by His finger on stone. They govern relationship with God (1-4) and with neighbor (5-10). | 
Sabbath (Hebrew: "Shabbat") | "Cessation/rest." The seventh day, set apart as holy. In Exodus, grounded in creation (God rested on the seventh day). In Deuteronomy, grounded in redemption (you were slaves — now rest). Both reasons are valid and complementary. | 
Face to Face      | Hebrew "panim b'fanim" — indicating directness and intimacy of communication. Not that they saw God's face (4:12 — "no similitude") but that He spoke directly, without intermediary, to the whole assembly. | 
Mediator          | Moses' role: standing between God and the people (v.5). The people could not bear God's direct voice; Moses received and transmitted. This points to Christ as the ultimate mediator (1 Timothy 2:5; Hebrews 8:6). | 
Vain (Hebrew: "shav") | Emptiness, falsehood, worthlessness. Taking God's name "in vain" means using it for empty, false, or worthless purposes — false oaths, casual profanity, or claiming His authority without His commission. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Not with our fathers... but with us" (v.3). The covenant is not ancient
   history — it is present reality. You stand before God today as surely as
   Israel stood at Sinai. His commands are addressed to you, now,
   personally.

2. "I am the LORD thy God, which brought thee out" (v.6). God's commands
   are grounded in His saving acts. He does not command strangers — He
   commands those He has already redeemed. Obedience is response to grace,
   not the means of earning it.

3. "Remember that thou wast a servant" (v.15). The Sabbath is not merely
   about rest — it is about remembering slavery and celebrating freedom.
   Give rest to others because you know what it is to have none.

4. "O that there were such an heart in them" (v.29). God's deepest desire
   is not mere external compliance but a heart that loves Him. The old
   covenant exposed the problem — the heart is unwilling. The new covenant
   provides the solution — God gives a new heart (Ezekiel 36:26).

5. "Ye shall not turn aside to the right hand or to the left" (v.32).
   Faithfulness is a straight path. Deviation in either direction — toward
   legalism or toward license — is departure from God's way.

6. The people's fear (vv.25-27). They heard God and were terrified. This is
   right — "the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom." But fear
   alone does not produce obedience. They needed what v.29 longs for: a
   transformed heart. Fear without love produces only temporary compliance.

------------------------------------

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-5/

  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

6.notes:
Deuteronomy - Chapter 6
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Moses commands Israel to love the Lord with all their heart, soul, and strength — the Shema — and to teach God's words diligently to their children.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Deuteronomy is the fifth and final book of the Pentateuch,
written at the end of the 40 years of wilderness wandering (approximately
1406 BC). Hebrew title: "Devarim" — "Words/Things." Greek title:
"Deuteronomion" — "Second Law" (a repetition/expansion of the Law for the
new generation). The book consists of Moses' farewell speeches to Israel on
the plains of Moab, just before they enter the Promised Land. Moses will
not enter with them — these are his final words to the nation he has led
for 40 years.

Historical Context: Chapter 6 contains the most important passage in
Judaism — the Shema (vv.4-5): "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God is one
LORD: And thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with
all thy soul, and with all thy might." This is the passage Jesus identified
as "the first and great commandment" (Matthew 22:37-38). The chapter flows
from the Ten Commandments (chapter 5) into their practical application:
love God totally, teach your children constantly, and do not forget God
when prosperity comes. Moses knows the greatest danger Israel will face is
not military enemies but spiritual amnesia — forgetting God in the comfort
of the Promised Land. The chapter establishes the pattern of faith
transmission: from heart (v.6) to home (v.7) to hand and eyes (v.8) to
doorposts and gates (v.9). God's word must saturate every dimension of
life. When the next generation asks "What mean these things?" the answer is
always the story of redemption (vv.20-25).

The Purpose of the Commandments (vv.1-3): These commandments are given "that
ye might do them in the land" (v.1) — they are for life in the Promised
Land. The purpose: fear God across generations — "thou, and thy son, and
thy son's son" (v.2). The result: prolonged days, well-being, and
multiplication "in the land that floweth with milk and honey" (v.3).
Obedience is not burdensome duty but the path to flourishing.

THE SHEMA (vv.4-5): "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God is one LORD: And
thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy
soul, and with all thy might." This is the foundational confession of
Israel's faith. "Hear" (Shema) — not merely auditory but responsive
hearing: hear and obey. "The LORD our God is one LORD" — YHWH alone is God;
He is undivided, unique, singular in His deity. "Love the LORD thy God" —
the first and greatest command is not a prohibition but a positive demand:
love. "With all thine heart" — the center of will and thought. "With all
thy soul" — the whole life/being. "With all thy might" — with all strength
and resources. Total, undivided, wholehearted devotion.

The Word in Every Sphere of Life (vv.6-9): "These words... shall be in
thine heart" (v.6) — internalized, not merely external. "Teach them
diligently unto thy children" (v.7) — Hebrew "shinnantam" means to sharpen,
to repeat, to impress. Talk of them constantly: sitting, walking, lying
down, rising up (v.7) — every posture, every moment. "Bind them... upon
thine hand" (v.8) — they govern your actions. "Frontlets between thine
eyes" (v.8) — they govern your vision/thinking. "Write them upon the posts
of thy house, and on thy gates" (v.9) — they govern your home and your
public life. Later Judaism literalized this with phylacteries (tefillin)
and mezuzot, but the original intent is comprehensive: God's word must
permeate everything.

The Danger of Prosperity (vv.10-15): When God brings them into the land —
cities they did not build, houses they did not fill, wells they did not
dig, vineyards they did not plant (vv.10-11) — "when thou shalt have eaten
and be full" (v.11) — "then beware lest thou forget the LORD" (v.12). This
is Moses' great warning: prosperity breeds forgetfulness. Full stomachs
produce empty memories. The antidote: fear God, serve Him, swear by His
name alone (v.13). Do not follow other gods (v.14) — "for the LORD thy God
is a jealous God among you" (v.15).

Do Not Test God (v.16): "Ye shall not tempt the LORD your God, as ye
tempted him in Massah." At Massah (Exodus 17:1-7), Israel tested God by
demanding: "Is the LORD among us, or not?" Testing God means demanding
proof of His presence or faithfulness rather than trusting His word. Jesus
quoted this verse when Satan tempted Him to jump from the temple (Matthew
4:7).

Teaching the Next Generation (vv.20-25): "When thy son asketh thee in time
to come, saying, What mean the testimonies...?" (v.20). The answer is not
abstract theology but redemptive history: "We were Pharaoh's bondmen in
Egypt; and the LORD brought us out" (v.21). He showed signs and wonders
(v.22). He brought us out to bring us in (v.23). He commanded us "for our
good always" (v.24). Obedience is "our righteousness" (v.25). The faith is
transmitted through story — the story of what God has done.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Moses speaks from the plains of Moab. These commands anticipate life "in the land" — "when thou comest into the land" (v.10).

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | Mark        | 12      | 29                 | Jesus quotes the Shema as the greatest commandment.
v.4              | 1 Corinthians | 8       | 4-6                | "There is no God but one."
v.5              | Matthew     | 22      | 37-38              | "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart... This is the great and first commandment."
v.5              | Luke        | 10      | 27                 | The lawyer quotes this in the Good Samaritan context.
v.7              | Deuteronomy | 11      | 19                 | Repeated command to teach children.
v.7              | Proverbs    | 22      | 6                  | "Train up a child in the way he should go."
v.8              | Matthew     | 23      | 5                  | Pharisees made their phylacteries broad (external show without internal reality).
v.10             | Joshua      | 24      | 13                 | "Cities that you did not build... vineyards that you did not plant."
v.12             | Deuteronomy | 8       | 11-14              | Expanded warning against forgetting in prosperity.
v.13             | Matthew     | 4       | 10                 | Jesus quotes this: "You shall worship the Lord your God and him only shall you serve."
v.16             | Matthew     | 4       | 7                  | Jesus quotes this when tempted to test God.
v.16             | Exodus      | 17      | 1-7                | The testing at Massah.
v.20             | Exodus      | 12      | 26                 | 27; 13:14 — Similar "when your son asks" passages regarding Passover.
v.25             | Romans      | 10      | 3-5                | Righteousness by law vs. righteousness by faith.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-6/
  Guzik emphasizes that the Shema is not merely a theological statement
  but a call to action — hearing that produces loving obedience. He notes
  that the command to love God with "all" (heart, soul, might) leaves no
  compartment of life outside God's claim. He highlights the danger of
  prosperity as the most relevant warning for Western Christians.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Love the Lord thy God with ALL. Not with part of your heart while the
  world has the rest. Not with your soul on Sundays while your strength
  belongs to mammon on weekdays. ALL. This is the command that no man has
  ever fully kept except One — and He kept it for us. Christ loved the
  Father with all His heart, all His soul, all His might. His perfect
  obedience is credited to all who believe."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4              | KJV         | "The LORD our God is one LORD" / ESV: "The LORD our God, the LORD is one" — Hebrew "YHWH Eloheinu YHWH echad." This can be translated multiple ways: "The LORD our God is one LORD" / "The LORD our God, the LORD is one" / "The LORD is our God, the LORD alone." "Echad" means one/unified/alone. The statement affirms both God's unity and His exclusivity.
v.5              | KJV         | "with all thy might" / ESV: "with all your might" — Hebrew "b'chol m'odecha." "M'od" literally means "very/exceedingly" — with all your "very-ness," your utmost capacity, your everything. Some render it "resources" or "abundance" — love God with everything you have and are.
v.7              | KJV         | "teach them diligently" / ESV: "teach them diligently" — Hebrew "v'shinnantam." From "shanan" = to sharpen, to whet. The image is of sharpening a blade by repeated strokes — teaching by constant repetition until the words are sharp and clear in the child's mind.
v.8              | KJV         | "frontlets between thine eyes" / ESV: "frontlets between your eyes" — Hebrew "totafot bein einecha." "Totafot" is a rare word, possibly meaning "bands" or "ornaments." Later Judaism interpreted this literally as tefillin (phylacteries) — small boxes containing Scripture worn on the forehead and arm during prayer.
v.12             | KJV         | "beware lest thou forget" / ESV: "take care lest you forget" — Hebrew "hishamer l'cha pen tishkach." "Hishamer" = guard yourself. "Pen" = lest. "Tishkach" = you forget. Active vigilance against spiritual amnesia.
v.16             | KJV         | "Ye shall not tempt the LORD" / ESV: "You shall not put the LORD your God to the test" — Hebrew "lo t'nassu et YHWH." "Nasah" = to test, to try, to prove. Testing God means demanding He prove Himself rather than trusting His revealed word.
v.25             | KJV         | "it shall be our righteousness" / ESV: "it will be righteousness for us" — Hebrew "ts'daqah tihyeh lanu." Obedience constitutes righteousness under the old covenant. Paul will later contrast this with righteousness by faith (Romans 10:5-6; Galatians 3:11-12).

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Shema             | Hebrew "Hear!" — the first word of verse 4 and the name given to this passage (6:4-9). Recited twice daily in Jewish practice. The foundational confession of Israel's faith: God is one, and He demands total love. | 
Echad             | Hebrew "one." Can mean numerically one, unified, or alone/ unique. In the Shema, it affirms God's unity (He is not divided), His uniqueness (there is no other), and His exclusivity (He alone is to be worshiped). | 
Frontlets/Tefillin | Phylacteries. Small leather boxes containing Scripture passages (Deut 6:4-9; 11:13-21; Exodus 13:1-10, 11-16), worn on the forehead and left arm during prayer. A literal interpretation of v.8. | 
Mezuzah           | A small case containing the Shema (Deut 6:4-9; 11:13-21) affixed to doorposts. A literal interpretation of v.9. The word means "doorpost." | 
Massah            | "Testing/Temptation" — the place where Israel tested God by demanding water and questioning His presence (Exodus 17:1-7). The name became a byword for faithless demanding of proof from God. | 
Diligently (Hebrew: "shanan") | To sharpen, to whet, to repeat. Teaching by constant repetition — like sharpening a blade with repeated strokes. Not casual mention but intentional, persistent instruction. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Hear, O Israel" (v.4). The Shema begins with hearing — but biblical
   hearing is never passive. To hear is to respond, to obey, to act. Are
   you hearing God's word, or merely listening?

2. "With all thine heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might"
   (v.5). God demands totality. Not 90% — all. No compartment of life is
   exempt from His claim. Love that holds something back is not the love
   God commands.

3. "Teach them diligently unto thy children" (v.7). Faith is transmitted
   through intentional, constant, life-integrated teaching. Not merely
   Sunday school but sitting, walking, lying down, rising up. Every moment
   is a teaching moment.

4. "When thou shalt have eaten and be full — then beware lest thou forget"
   (vv.11-12). Prosperity is more dangerous than poverty. Want drives us to
   God; abundance tempts us to forget Him. The full stomach is the enemy of
   the grateful heart.

5. "He brought us out... that he might bring us in" (v.23). Salvation has a
   purpose beyond escape. God does not merely save FROM — He saves FOR. He
   brought Israel out of Egypt to bring them into the land. He saves you
   from sin to bring you into His purposes.

6. "For our good always" (v.24). God's commands are not arbitrary
   restrictions imposed by a tyrant. They are the loving instructions of a
   Father who knows what leads to life. Obedience is always "for our good."

------------------------------------

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-6/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

7.ESV:
1. “When the LORD your God brings you into the land that you are entering to take possession of it, and clears away many nations before you, the Hittites, the Girgashites, the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites, seven nations more numerous and mightier than you,
2. and when the LORD your God gives them over to you, and you defeat them, then you must devote them to complete destruction. You shall make no covenant with them and show no mercy to them.
3. You shall not intermarry with them, giving your daughters to their sons or taking their daughters for your sons,
4. for they would turn away your sons from following me, to serve other gods. Then the anger of the LORD would be kindled against you, and he would destroy you quickly.
5. But thus shall you deal with them: you shall break down their altars and dash in pieces their pillars and chop down their Asherim and burn their carved images with fire.
6. “For you are a people holy to the LORD your God. The LORD your God has chosen you to be a people for his treasured possession, out of all the peoples who are on the face of the earth.
7. It was not because you were more in number than any other people that the LORD set his love on you and chose you, for you were the fewest of all peoples,
8. but it is because the LORD loves you and is keeping the oath that he swore to your fathers, that the LORD has brought you out with a mighty hand and redeemed you from the house of slavery, from the hand of Pharaoh king of Egypt.
9. Know therefore that the LORD your God is God, the faithful God who keeps covenant and steadfast love with those who love him and keep his commandments, to a thousand generations,
10. and repays to their face those who hate him, by destroying them. He will not be slack with one who hates him. He will repay him to his face.
11. You shall therefore be careful to do the commandment and the statutes and the rules that I command you today.
12. “And because you listen to these rules and keep and do them, the LORD your God will keep with you the covenant and the steadfast love that he swore to your fathers.
13. He will love you, bless you, and multiply you. He will also bless the fruit of your womb and the fruit of your ground, your grain and your wine and your oil, the increase of your herds and the young of your flock, in the land that he swore to your fathers to give you.
14. You shall be blessed above all peoples. There shall not be male or female barren among you or among your livestock.
15. And the LORD will take away from you all sickness, and none of the evil diseases of Egypt, which you knew, will he inflict on you, but he will lay them on all who hate you.
16. And you shall consume all the peoples that the LORD your God will give over to you. Your eye shall not pity them, neither shall you serve their gods, for that would be a snare to you.
17. “If you say in your heart, ‘These nations are greater than I. How can I dispossess them?’
18. you shall not be afraid of them but you shall remember what the LORD your God did to Pharaoh and to all Egypt,
19. the great trials that your eyes saw, the signs, the wonders, the mighty hand, and the outstretched arm, by which the LORD your God brought you out. So will the LORD your God do to all the peoples of whom you are afraid.
20. Moreover, the LORD your God will send hornets among them, until those who are left and hide themselves from you are destroyed.
21. You shall not be in dread of them, for the LORD your God is in your midst, a great and awesome God.
22. The LORD your God will clear away these nations before you little by little. You may not make an end of them at once, lest the wild beasts grow too numerous for you.
23. But the LORD your God will give them over to you and throw them into great confusion, until they are destroyed.
24. And he will give their kings into your hand, and you shall make their name perish from under heaven. No one shall be able to stand against you until you have destroyed them.
25. The carved images of their gods you shall burn with fire. You shall not covet the silver or the gold that is on them or take it for yourselves, lest you be ensnared by it, for it is an abomination to the LORD your God.
26. And you shall not bring an abominable thing into your house and become devoted to destruction like it. You shall utterly detest and abhor it, for it is devoted to destruction.
7.KJV:
1. When the LORD thy God shall bring thee into the land whither thou goest to possess it, and hath cast out many nations before thee, the Hittites, and the Girgashites, and the Amorites, and the Canaanites, and the Perizzites, and the Hivites, and the Jebusites, seven nations greater and mightier than thou;
2. And when the LORD thy God shall deliver them before thee; thou shalt smite them, and utterly destroy them; thou shalt make no covenant with them, nor shew mercy unto them:
3. Neither shalt thou make marriages with them; thy daughter thou shalt not give unto his son, nor his daughter shalt thou take unto thy son.
4. For they will turn away thy son from following me, that they may serve other gods: so will the anger of the LORD be kindled against you, and destroy thee suddenly.
5. But thus shall ye deal with them; ye shall destroy their altars, and break down their images, and cut down their groves, and burn their graven images with fire.
6. For thou art an holy people unto the LORD thy God: the LORD thy God hath chosen thee to be a special people unto himself, above all people that are upon the face of the earth.
7. The LORD did not set his love upon you, nor choose you, because ye were more in number than any people; for ye were the fewest of all people:
8. But because the LORD loved you, and because he would keep the oath which he had sworn unto your fathers, hath the LORD brought you out with a mighty hand, and redeemed you out of the house of bondmen, from the hand of Pharaoh king of Egypt.
9. Know therefore that the LORD thy God, he is God, the faithful God, which keepeth covenant and mercy with them that love him and keep his commandments to a thousand generations;
10. And repayeth them that hate him to their face, to destroy them: he will not be slack to him that hateth him, he will repay him to his face.
11. Thou shalt therefore keep the commandments, and the statutes, and the judgments, which I command thee this day, to do them.
12. Wherefore it shall come to pass, if ye hearken to these judgments, and keep, and do them, that the LORD thy God shall keep unto thee the covenant and the mercy which he sware unto thy fathers:
13. And he will love thee, and bless thee, and multiply thee: he will also bless the fruit of thy womb, and the fruit of thy land, thy corn, and thy wine, and thine oil, the increase of thy kine, and the flocks of thy sheep, in the land which he sware unto thy fathers to give thee.
14. Thou shalt be blessed above all people: there shall not be male or female barren among you, or among your cattle.
15. And the LORD will take away from thee all sickness, and will put none of the evil diseases of Egypt, which thou knowest, upon thee; but will lay them upon all them that hate thee.
16. And thou shalt consume all the people which the LORD thy God shall deliver thee; thine eye shall have no pity upon them: neither shalt thou serve their gods; for that will be a snare unto thee.
17. If thou shalt say in thine heart, These nations are more than I; how can I dispossess them?
18. Thou shalt not be afraid of them: but shalt well remember what the LORD thy God did unto Pharaoh, and unto all Egypt;
19. The great temptations which thine eyes saw, and the signs, and the wonders, and the mighty hand, and the stretched out arm, whereby the LORD thy God brought thee out: so shall the LORD thy God do unto all the people of whom thou art afraid.
20. Moreover the LORD thy God will send the hornet among them, until they that are left, and hide themselves from thee, be destroyed.
21. Thou shalt not be affrighted at them: for the LORD thy God is among you, a mighty God and terrible.
22. And the LORD thy God will put out those nations before thee by little and little: thou mayest not consume them at once, lest the beasts of the field increase upon thee.
23. But the LORD thy God shall deliver them unto thee, and shall destroy them with a mighty destruction, until they be destroyed.
24. And he shall deliver their kings into thine hand, and thou shalt destroy their name from under heaven: there shall no man be able to stand before thee, until thou have destroyed them.
25. The graven images of their gods shall ye burn with fire: thou shalt not desire the silver or gold that is on them, nor take it unto thee, lest thou be snared therein: for it is an abomination to the LORD thy God.
26. Neither shalt thou bring an abomination into thine house, lest thou be a cursed thing like it: but thou shalt utterly detest it, and thou shalt utterly abhor it; for it is a cursed thing.

7.NET:
1. When the Lord your God brings you to the land that you are going to occupy and forces out many nations before you—Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites, seven nations more numerous and powerful than you—
2. and he delivers them over to you and you attack them, you must utterly annihilate them. Make no treaty with them and show them no mercy!
3. You must not intermarry with them. Do not give your daughters to their sons or take their daughters for your sons,
4. for they will turn your sons away from me to worship other gods. Then the anger of the Lord will erupt against you and he will quickly destroy you.
5. Instead, this is what you must do to them: You must tear down their altars, shatter their sacred pillars, cut down their sacred Asherah poles, and burn up their idols.
6. For you are a people holy to the Lord your God. He has chosen you to be his people, prized above all others on the face of the earth.
7. It is not because you were more numerous than all the other peoples that the Lord favored and chose you—for in fact you were the least numerous of all peoples.
8. Rather it is because of his love for you and his faithfulness to the promise he solemnly vowed to your ancestors that the Lord brought you out with great power, redeeming you from the place of slavery, from the power of Pharaoh king of Egypt.
9. So realize that the Lord your God is the true God, the faithful God who keeps covenant faithfully with those who love him and keep his commandments, to a thousand generations,
10. but who pays back those who hate him as they deserve and destroys them. He will not ignore those who hate him but will repay them as they deserve!
11. So keep the commandments, statutes, and ordinances that I today am commanding you to do.
12. If you obey these ordinances and are careful to do them, the Lord your God will faithfully keep covenant with you as he promised your ancestors.
13. He will love and bless you, and make you numerous. He will bless you with many children, with the produce of your soil, your grain, your new wine, your olive oil, the offspring of your oxen, and the young of your flocks in the land that he promised your ancestors to give you.
14. You will be blessed beyond all peoples; there will be no barrenness among you or your livestock.
15. The Lord will protect you from all sickness, and you will not experience any of the terrible diseases that you knew in Egypt; instead he will inflict them on all those who hate you.
16. You must destroy all the people whom the Lord your God is about to deliver over to you; you must not pity them or worship their gods, for that will be a snare to you.
17. If you think, “These nations are more numerous than I—how can I dispossess them?”
18. you must not fear them. You must carefully recall what the Lord your God did to Pharaoh and all Egypt,
19. the great judgments you saw, the signs and wonders, the strength and power by which he brought you out—thus the Lord your God will do to all the people you fear.
20. Furthermore, the Lord your God will release hornets among them until the very last ones who hide from you perish.
21. You must not tremble in their presence, for the Lord your God, who is present among you, is a great and awesome God.
22. He, the God who leads you, will expel the nations little by little. You will not be allowed to destroy them all at once lest the wild animals overrun you.
23. The Lord your God will give them over to you; he will throw them into a great panic until they are destroyed.
24. He will hand over their kings to you, and you will erase their very names from memory. Nobody will be able to resist you until you destroy them.
25. You must burn the images of their gods, but do not covet the silver and gold that covers them so much that you take it for yourself and thus become ensnared by it; for it is abhorrent to the Lord your God.
26. You must not bring any abhorrent thing into your house and thereby become an object of divine wrath along with it. You must absolutely detest and abhor it, for it is an object of divine wrath.
