  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

17.ESV:
1. “You shall not sacrifice to the LORD your God an ox or a sheep in which is a blemish, any defect whatever, for that is an abomination to the LORD your God.
2. “If there is found among you, within any of your towns that the LORD your God is giving you, a man or woman who does what is evil in the sight of the LORD your God, in transgressing his covenant,
3. and has gone and served other gods and worshiped them, or the sun or the moon or any of the host of heaven, which I have forbidden,
4. and it is told you and you hear of it, then you shall inquire diligently, and if it is true and certain that such an abomination has been done in Israel,
5. then you shall bring out to your gates that man or woman who has done this evil thing, and you shall stone that man or woman to death with stones.
6. On the evidence of two witnesses or of three witnesses the one who is to die shall be put to death; a person shall not be put to death on the evidence of one witness.
7. The hand of the witnesses shall be first against him to put him to death, and afterward the hand of all the people. So you shall purge the evil from your midst.
8. “If any case arises requiring decision between one kind of homicide and another, one kind of legal right and another, or one kind of assault and another, any case within your towns that is too difficult for you, then you shall arise and go up to the place that the LORD your God will choose.
9. And you shall come to the Levitical priests and to the judge who is in office in those days, and you shall consult them, and they shall declare to you the decision.
10. Then you shall do according to what they declare to you from that place that the LORD will choose. And you shall be careful to do according to all that they direct you.
11. According to the instructions that they give you, and according to the decision which they pronounce to you, you shall do. You shall not turn aside from the verdict that they declare to you, either to the right hand or to the left.
12. The man who acts presumptuously by not obeying the priest who stands to minister there before the LORD your God, or the judge, that man shall die. So you shall purge the evil from Israel.
13. And all the people shall hear and fear and not act presumptuously again.
14. “When you come to the land that the LORD your God is giving you, and you possess it and dwell in it and then say, ‘I will set a king over me, like all the nations that are around me,’
15. you may indeed set a king over you whom the LORD your God will choose. One from among your brothers you shall set as king over you. You may not put a foreigner over you, who is not your brother.
16. Only he must not acquire many horses for himself or cause the people to return to Egypt in order to acquire many horses, since the LORD has said to you, ‘You shall never return that way again.’
17. And he shall not acquire many wives for himself, lest his heart turn away, nor shall he acquire for himself excessive silver and gold.
18. “And when he sits on the throne of his kingdom, he shall write for himself in a book a copy of this law, approved by the Levitical priests.
19. And it shall be with him, and he shall read in it all the days of his life, that he may learn to fear the LORD his God by keeping all the words of this law and these statutes, and doing them,
20. that his heart may not be lifted up above his brothers, and that he may not turn aside from the commandment, either to the right hand or to the left, so that he may continue long in his kingdom, he and his children, in Israel.
17.KJV:
1. Thou shalt not sacrifice unto the LORD thy God any bullock, or sheep, wherein is blemish, or any evilfavouredness: for that is an abomination unto the LORD thy God.
2. If there be found among you, within any of thy gates which the LORD thy God giveth thee, man or woman, that hath wrought wickedness in the sight of the LORD thy God, in transgressing his covenant,
3. And hath gone and served other gods, and worshipped them, either the sun, or moon, or any of the host of heaven, which I have not commanded;
4. And it be told thee, and thou hast heard of it, and enquired diligently, and, behold, it be true, and the thing certain, that such abomination is wrought in Israel:
5. Then shalt thou bring forth that man or that woman, which have committed that wicked thing, unto thy gates, even that man or that woman, and shalt stone them with stones, till they die.
6. At the mouth of two witnesses, or three witnesses, shall he that is worthy of death be put to death; but at the mouth of one witness he shall not be put to death.
7. The hands of the witnesses shall be first upon him to put him to death, and afterward the hands of all the people. So thou shalt put the evil away from among you.
8. If there arise a matter too hard for thee in judgment, between blood and blood, between plea and plea, and between stroke and stroke, being matters of controversy within thy gates: then shalt thou arise, and get thee up into the place which the LORD thy God shall choose;
9. And thou shalt come unto the priests the Levites, and unto the judge that shall be in those days, and enquire; and they shall shew thee the sentence of judgment:
10. And thou shalt do according to the sentence, which they of that place which the LORD shall choose shall shew thee; and thou shalt observe to do according to all that they inform thee:
11. According to the sentence of the law which they shall teach thee, and according to the judgment which they shall tell thee, thou shalt do: thou shalt not decline from the sentence which they shall shew thee, to the right hand, nor to the left.
12. And the man that will do presumptuously, and will not hearken unto the priest that standeth to minister there before the LORD thy God, or unto the judge, even that man shall die: and thou shalt put away the evil from Israel.
13. And all the people shall hear, and fear, and do no more presumptuously.
14. When thou art come unto the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee, and shalt possess it, and shalt dwell therein, and shalt say, I will set a king over me, like as all the nations that are about me;
15. Thou shalt in any wise set him king over thee, whom the LORD thy God shall choose: one from among thy brethren shalt thou set king over thee: thou mayest not set a stranger over thee, which is not thy brother.
16. But he shall not multiply horses to himself, nor cause the people to return to Egypt, to the end that he should multiply horses: forasmuch as the LORD hath said unto you, Ye shall henceforth return no more that way.
17. Neither shall he multiply wives to himself, that his heart turn not away: neither shall he greatly multiply to himself silver and gold.
18. And it shall be, when he sitteth upon the throne of his kingdom, that he shall write him a copy of this law in a book out of that which is before the priests the Levites:
19. And it shall be with him, and he shall read therein all the days of his life: that he may learn to fear the LORD his God, to keep all the words of this law and these statutes, to do them:
20. That his heart be not lifted up above his brethren, and that he turn not aside from the commandment, to the right hand, or to the left: to the end that he may prolong his days in his kingdom, he, and his children, in the midst of Israel.

17.notes:
Deuteronomy - Chapter 17
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God gives laws for judges, forbids idolatry, and prescribes rules for Israel's future king — he must write a copy of the law and read it daily.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Deuteronomy is the fifth and final book of the Pentateuch,
written at the end of the 40 years of wilderness wandering (approximately
1406 BC). Hebrew title: "Devarim" — "Words/Things." Greek title:
"Deuteronomion" — "Second Law" (a repetition/expansion of the Law for the
new generation). The book consists of Moses' farewell speeches to Israel on
the plains of Moab, just before they enter the Promised Land. Moses will
not enter with them — these are his final words to the nation he has led
for 40 years.

Historical Context: Chapter 17 addresses three critical areas of
governance: (1) the prosecution of idolaters with proper judicial
safeguards (vv.1-7), (2) the supreme court for difficult cases (vv.8-13),
and (3) the law of the king (vv.14-20). The chapter opens with a
prohibition against blemished sacrifices (v.1) — connecting worship
integrity to judicial integrity. The idolatry laws (vv.2-7) establish the
crucial requirement of multiple witnesses: no one may be executed on the
testimony of a single witness (v.6). The witnesses must cast the first
stones (v.7) — ensuring personal accountability for accusations. The
supreme court (vv.8-13) provides for cases too difficult for local judges:
they go to the central sanctuary where Levitical priests and the presiding
judge render binding decisions. The law of the king (vv.14-20) is
remarkable: Moses anticipates Israel's future request for a king (fulfilled
in 1 Samuel 8) and provides constitutional limits. The king must be God's
choice, an Israelite (v.15). He must NOT multiply horses (military power),
wives (political alliances), or silver and gold (wealth) (vv.16-17). Most
remarkably, he must write his own copy of the law and read it daily
(vv.18-19). The king is not above the law — he is under it. His heart
must not be "lifted up above his brethren" (v.20). Israel's king is a
brother under God's law, not an absolute monarch. Solomon violated every
prohibition in vv.16-17 (1 Kings 10-11), and his kingdom was torn apart.

Blemished Sacrifices Forbidden (v.1): "Thou shalt not sacrifice unto the
LORD thy God any bullock, or sheep, wherein is blemish, or any
evilfavouredness" (v.1). This is "an abomination unto the LORD thy God"
(v.1). God deserves the best, not the defective. A blemished sacrifice
insults God — it says He is not worth your best.

Prosecution of Idolaters (vv.2-7): "If there be found among you... man or
woman, that hath wrought wickedness... in transgressing his covenant" (v.2).
The specific crime: serving "other gods... either the sun, or moon, or any
of the host of heaven" (v.3) — astral worship, common in the ancient Near
East. Process: "enquired diligently" (v.4) — thorough investigation. If
confirmed: "stone them with stones, till they die" (v.5). Critical
safeguard: "At the mouth of two witnesses, or three witnesses, shall he
that is worthy of death be put to death; but at the mouth of one witness
he shall not be put to death" (v.6). The witnesses must throw first (v.7)
— they bear personal responsibility for the execution. "So thou shalt put
the evil away from among you" (v.7).

The Supreme Court for Difficult Cases (vv.8-13): "If there arise a matter
too hard for thee in judgment" (v.8) — cases involving homicide, legal
rights, or assault that are too complex for local courts. Solution: "arise,
and get thee up into the place which the LORD thy God shall choose" (v.8).
Consult "the priests the Levites, and unto the judge that shall be in
those days" (v.9). Their decision is binding: "thou shalt do according to
the sentence" (v.10). "Thou shalt not decline from the sentence... to the
right hand, nor to the left" (v.11). Penalty for contempt of court: "the
man that will do presumptuously, and will not hearken unto the priest...
or unto the judge, even that man shall die" (v.12). Purpose: "all the
people shall hear, and fear, and do no more presumptuously" (v.13).

The Law of the King (vv.14-20): "When thou art come unto the land... and
shalt say, I will set a king over me, like as all the nations" (v.14).
Moses anticipates the request. God permits it with conditions: "Thou shalt
in any wise set him king over thee, whom the LORD thy God shall choose"
(v.15). He must be an Israelite — "thou mayest not set a stranger over
thee" (v.15). Three prohibitions: (1) "he shall not multiply horses to
himself, nor cause the people to return to Egypt" (v.16) — no military
buildup through Egyptian alliances; (2) "Neither shall he multiply wives
to himself, that his heart turn not away" (v.17) — no political marriages
that introduce foreign gods; (3) "neither shall he greatly multiply to
himself silver and gold" (v.17) — no excessive wealth accumulation. The
king's primary duty: "he shall write him a copy of this law in a book"
(v.18). "He shall read therein all the days of his life" (v.19). Purpose:
"that he may learn to fear the LORD his God" (v.19), "that his heart be
not lifted up above his brethren" (v.20), and "that he turn not aside from
the commandment" (v.20). Promise: "he may prolong his days in his kingdom,
he, and his children" (v.20).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Moses speaks from the plains of Moab. The future king will rule "in the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee" (v.14) — laws for Canaan's governance.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Malachi     | 1       | 8                  | ,13-14 — God rebukes blemished sacrifices: "Present that to your governor; will he accept you?"
v.1              | 1 Peter     | 1       | 19                 | Christ: "a lamb without blemish and without spot."
v.3              | 2 Kings     | 21      | 3-5                | Manasseh worshiped the host of heaven.
v.6              | Numbers     | 35      | 30                 | Two witnesses required for murder conviction.
v.6              | Matthew     | 18      | 16                 | "In the mouth of two or three witnesses every word may be established."
v.6              | 2 Corinthians | 13      | 1                  | Paul applies the two-witness rule.
v.7              | Deuteronomy | 13      | 9                  | The accuser's hand first in execution.
v.7              | John        | 8       | 7                  | "He that is without sin among you, let him first cast a stone."
v.8              | 2 Chronicles | 19      | 8-11               | Jehoshaphat establishes the supreme court.
v.14             | 1 Samuel    | 8       | 5                  | "Make us a king to judge us like all the nations."
v.15             | 1 Samuel    | 9       | 17                 | ; 16:1,12 — God chooses Saul, then David.
v.16             | 1 Kings     | 10      | 26-29              | Solomon multiplied horses from Egypt.
v.17             | 1 Kings     | 11      | 1-4                | Solomon multiplied wives; his heart turned away.
v.17             | 1 Kings     | 10      | 14-23              | Solomon multiplied silver and gold.
v.18             | Joshua      | 1       | 8                  | "This Book of the Law shall not depart from your mouth, but you shall meditate on it day and night."
v.18             | Psalm       | 1       | 2                  | "On his law he meditates day and night."
v.20             | Philippians | 2       | 3                  | "In lowliness of mind let each esteem other better than themselves."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-17/
  Guzik emphasizes that the law of the king is a constitutional monarchy
  — the king is under God's law, not above it. He notes that Solomon
  violated all three prohibitions (horses, wives, gold) and suffered the
  consequences. The requirement to copy and read the law daily made the
  king a student of Scripture first and a ruler second.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "The king must write out the law with his own hand and read it every day
  of his life. What a prescription for leadership! Not military strategy,
  not political cunning, not economic theory — but the daily reading of
  God's word. The leader who neglects Scripture will inevitably multiply
  what God forbids. Solomon is the proof: the wisest man who ever lived
  destroyed his kingdom because he stopped listening to God's word."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "any evilfavouredness" / ESV: "any defect whatever" — Hebrew "kol davar ra." Literally "any evil thing/matter." KJV's archaic "evilfavouredness" means "ugly defect." ESV generalizes to "any defect." The point: no imperfection of any kind in what is offered to God.
v.3              | KJV         | "which I have not commanded" / ESV: "which I have forbidden" — Hebrew "asher lo tzivviti." Literally "which I have not commanded." KJV is literal; ESV interprets (what God has not commanded is effectively forbidden in worship). Both are valid readings.
v.8              | KJV         | "between blood and blood, between plea and plea, and between stroke and stroke" / ESV: "between one kind of homicide and another, one kind of legal right and another, or one kind of assault and another" — Hebrew "bein dam l'dam, bein din l'din, u-vein nega l'nega." KJV is literal; ESV interprets the legal categories. "Dam" = blood (homicide cases). "Din" = judgment/plea (civil cases). "Nega" = stroke/plague (assault/injury cases).
v.12             | KJV         | "do presumptuously" / ESV: "acts presumptuously" — Hebrew "ya'aseh v'zadon." "Zadon" = presumption, arrogance, insolence. Acting in deliberate defiance of the court's authority. Not ignorance but willful rebellion against judicial authority.
v.15             | KJV         | "one from among thy brethren" / ESV: "One from among your brothers" — Hebrew "mi-qerev acheicha." The king must be an Israelite — a brother, not a foreigner. This ensures the king shares the covenant identity and obligations of the people.
v.16             | KJV         | "multiply horses to himself" / ESV: "acquire many horses for himself" — Hebrew "lo yarbeh lo susim." Horses = military power (chariots, cavalry). Multiplying horses meant trusting in military might rather than God. Egypt was the primary source of horses — hence the prohibition against returning there.
v.18             | KJV         | "a copy of this law" / ESV: "a copy of this law" — Hebrew "mishneh ha-torah ha-zot." "Mishneh" = copy, duplicate. The Greek translation (LXX) rendered this "deuteronomion" (second law) — giving the book its name "Deuteronomy." The king personally copies the law — not delegated to a scribe.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Two               | Witness Rule — The requirement that no person be convicted of a capital crime on the testimony of a single witness. At least two (preferably three) witnesses are required. This prevents false accusations and ensures justice. Adopted into NT church discipline (Matthew 18:16). | 
Presumptuous Sin  | Hebrew "zadon" — deliberate, willful defiance of authority. Distinguished from sins of ignorance. The presumptuous person knowingly rebels against God's established order. Punishable by death (v.12). | 
Law of the King   | The constitutional limits placed on Israel's future monarch (vv.14-20). The king is chosen by God, must be Israelite, must not multiply horses/wives/gold, and must copy and read the law daily. A constitutional monarchy under God's law. | 
Multiply          | Hebrew "rabah" — to increase greatly, accumulate in excess. Applied to horses (military power), wives (political alliances), and silver/gold (wealth). The king must not accumulate power, pleasure, or possessions beyond what God permits. | 
Copy of the Law   | Hebrew "mishneh ha-torah" — the king's personal handwritten copy of God's law. The act of copying ensures intimate familiarity with every word. The daily reading ensures ongoing submission to God's authority. | 
Host of Heaven    | The sun, moon, and stars worshiped as deities in ancient Near Eastern religion. Astral worship was widespread in Mesopotamia and Canaan. Strictly forbidden for Israel — these are created things, not gods (Deuteronomy 4:19). | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. No blemished sacrifice (v.1). God deserves our best — not our leftovers,
   not our defective offerings. This applies to time, energy, attention,
   and resources given to God. Offering God what costs us nothing is an
   abomination (2 Samuel 24:24).

2. The two-witness rule (v.6). God builds safeguards into justice. No
   person's life depends on a single accusation. This protects against
   false testimony, personal vendettas, and hasty judgment. Justice
   requires patience and corroboration.

3. The king under the law (vv.18-20). The highest human authority is still
   under God's authority. No leader is above Scripture. The king's primary
   qualification is not charisma or competence but submission to God's
   word. Leaders who neglect Scripture inevitably abuse power.

4. "He shall read therein all the days of his life" (v.19). Daily Scripture
   reading is not optional for leaders — or for any believer. The king who
   stops reading becomes the king who starts multiplying what God forbids.
   Neglect of the word precedes every other failure.

5. "That his heart be not lifted up above his brethren" (v.20). Power tends
   toward pride. The antidote is daily immersion in God's word, which
   reminds the leader that he is a brother, not a lord. Humility is
   maintained by the word, not by willpower.

6. Solomon's warning. Every prohibition in vv.16-17 was violated by
   Solomon: horses from Egypt (1 Kings 10:28), 700 wives and 300 concubines
   (1 Kings 11:3), silver and gold beyond measure (1 Kings 10:14-23). The
   result: his heart turned away (1 Kings 11:4) and his kingdom was torn (1
   Kings 11:11). The law of the king is not theoretical — it is
   prophetically precise.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Deuteronomy Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/deuteronomy/

David Guzik — Deuteronomy 17 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-17/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

18.ESV:
1. “The Levitical priests, all the tribe of Levi, shall have no portion or inheritance with Israel. They shall eat the LORD’s food offerings as their inheritance.
2. They shall have no inheritance among their brothers; the LORD is their inheritance, as he promised them.
3. And this shall be the priests’ due from the people, from those offering a sacrifice, whether an ox or a sheep: they shall give to the priest the shoulder and the two cheeks and the stomach.
4. The firstfruits of your grain, of your wine and of your oil, and the first fleece of your sheep, you shall give him.
5. For the LORD your God has chosen him out of all your tribes to stand and minister in the name of the LORD, him and his sons for all time.
6. “And if a Levite comes from any of your towns out of all Israel, where he lives—and he may come when he desires—to the place that the LORD will choose,
7. and ministers in the name of the LORD his God, like all his fellow Levites who stand to minister there before the LORD,
8. then he may have equal portions to eat, besides what he receives from the sale of his patrimony.
9. “When you come into the land that the LORD your God is giving you, you shall not learn to follow the abominable practices of those nations.
10. There shall not be found among you anyone who burns his son or his daughter as an offering, anyone who practices divination or tells fortunes or interprets omens, or a sorcerer
11. or a charmer or a medium or a necromancer or one who inquires of the dead,
12. for whoever does these things is an abomination to the LORD. And because of these abominations the LORD your God is driving them out before you.
13. You shall be blameless before the LORD your God,
14. for these nations, which you are about to dispossess, listen to fortune-tellers and to diviners. But as for you, the LORD your God has not allowed you to do this.
15. “The LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you, from your brothers—it is to him you shall listen—
16. just as you desired of the LORD your God at Horeb on the day of the assembly, when you said, ‘Let me not hear again the voice of the LORD my God or see this great fire any more, lest I die.’
17. And the LORD said to me, ‘They are right in what they have spoken.
18. I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their brothers. And I will put my words in his mouth, and he shall speak to them all that I command him.
19. And whoever will not listen to my words that he shall speak in my name, I myself will require it of him.
20. But the prophet who presumes to speak a word in my name that I have not commanded him to speak, or who speaks in the name of other gods, that same prophet shall die.’
21. And if you say in your heart, ‘How may we know the word that the LORD has not spoken?’—
22. when a prophet speaks in the name of the LORD, if the word does not come to pass or come true, that is a word that the LORD has not spoken; the prophet has spoken it presumptuously. You need not be afraid of him.

16. This is according to all that you desired of Yahweh your God in Horeb in the day of the assembly, saying, “Let me not hear again Yahweh my God’s voice, neither let me see this great fire any more, that I not die.”

19. It shall happen, that whoever will not listen to my words which he shall speak in my name, I will require it of him.

18.notes:
Deuteronomy - Chapter 18
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God provides for the Levites, forbids occult practices, and promises a Prophet like Moses whom Israel must hear — ultimately fulfilled in Christ.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Deuteronomy is the fifth and final book of the Pentateuch,
written at the end of the 40 years of wilderness wandering (approximately
1406 BC). Hebrew title: "Devarim" — "Words/Things." Greek title:
"Deuteronomion" — "Second Law" (a repetition/expansion of the Law for the
new generation). The book consists of Moses' farewell speeches to Israel on
the plains of Moab, just before they enter the Promised Land. Moses will
not enter with them — these are his final words to the nation he has led
for 40 years.

Historical Context: Chapter 18 is one of the most theologically
significant chapters in Deuteronomy, containing the great Messianic
prophecy of "the Prophet like Moses" (vv.15-19). The chapter addresses
three topics: (1) provision for the Levitical priests (vv.1-8), (2)
prohibition of occult practices (vv.9-14), and (3) the promise of the
Prophet to come (vv.15-22). The logic flows naturally: the Levites serve
as God's ministers (vv.1-8); the nations consult demonic sources for
guidance (vv.9-14); but Israel will receive divine guidance through a
Prophet whom God Himself will raise up (vv.15-19). The occult practices
listed (vv.10-11) represent the full range of pagan attempts to access
supernatural knowledge: child sacrifice, divination, fortune-telling,
interpreting omens, sorcery, charming, consulting mediums, wizardry, and
necromancy (consulting the dead). These are not merely forbidden — they
are the very reason God is driving out the Canaanites (v.12). Israel's
alternative is not ignorance but revelation: God will speak through His
Prophet. The prophecy of vv.15-18 is explicitly identified as fulfilled in
Christ (Acts 3:22-23; 7:37). Jesus is THE Prophet like Moses — a mediator
between God and man, who speaks God's words with God's authority. The
chapter closes with the test of a prophet: if his word does not come to
pass, he has spoken presumptuously (vv.21-22). Combined with chapter 13
(the message must align with God's revealed truth), this gives Israel two
tests: (1) Does the message lead to God? (2) Does the prediction come
true?

Provision for Levitical Priests (vv.1-8): "The priests the Levites, and
all the tribe of Levi, shall have no part nor inheritance with Israel"
(v.1). They have no land allotment. Their provision: "they shall eat the
offerings of the LORD made by fire, and his inheritance" (v.1). "The LORD
is their inheritance" (v.2). The priests' due from sacrifices: "the
shoulder, and the two cheeks, and the maw" (KJV) / "the shoulder and the
two cheeks and the stomach" (ESV) (v.3). Also: firstfruits of grain, wine,
oil, and the first fleece of sheep (v.4). Why? "For the LORD thy God hath
chosen him out of all thy tribes, to stand to minister in the name of the
LORD, him and his sons for ever" (v.5). A Levite from any town may come to
the central sanctuary and minister with equal rights (vv.6-8) — he
receives equal portions "beside that which cometh of the sale of his
patrimony" (v.8).

Forbidden Occult Practices (vv.9-14): "Thou shalt not learn to do after
the abominations of those nations" (v.9). The comprehensive list: (1)
"maketh his son or his daughter to pass through the fire" — child
sacrifice (v.10); (2) "useth divination" — seeking hidden knowledge
through rituals (v.10); (3) "observer of times" (KJV) / "tells fortunes"
(ESV) — reading signs/omens (v.10); (4) "enchanter" (KJV) / "interprets
omens" (ESV) (v.10); (5) "witch" (KJV) / "sorcerer" (ESV) (v.10); (6)
"charmer" — one who binds with spells (v.11); (7) "consulter with familiar
spirits" (KJV) / "medium" (ESV) (v.11); (8) "wizard" (KJV) / "necromancer"
(ESV) (v.11); (9) one who "inquires of the dead" (v.11). "For all that do
these things are an abomination unto the LORD" (v.12). This is WHY God
drives out the Canaanites — "because of these abominations" (v.12). "Thou
shalt be perfect with the LORD thy God" (KJV) / "You shall be blameless
before the LORD your God" (ESV) (v.13). The nations listen to diviners;
"but as for thee, the LORD thy God hath not suffered thee so to do" (v.14).

THE PROPHET LIKE MOSES (vv.15-19): "The LORD thy God will raise up unto
thee a Prophet from the midst of thee, of thy brethren, like unto me; unto
him ye shall hearken" (v.15). This is Moses speaking — the Prophet will be
like Moses: (1) from among Israel ("thy brethren"), (2) a mediator between
God and man, (3) speaking God's words with God's authority. Background:
at Horeb/Sinai, the people feared God's direct voice and asked for a
mediator (v.16). God approved: "They have well spoken" (v.17). God's
promise: "I will raise them up a Prophet from among their brethren, like
unto thee, and will put my words in his mouth; and he shall speak unto
them all that I shall command him" (v.18). Warning: "whosoever will not
hearken unto my words which he shall speak in my name, I will require it
of him" (v.19). Rejecting this Prophet brings God's judgment.

Testing a Prophet (vv.20-22): Two categories of false prophets: (1) one
who "presume to speak a word in my name, which I have not commanded him"
(v.20); (2) one who "shall speak in the name of other gods" (v.20). Both
shall die. The test: "When a prophet speaketh in the name of the LORD, if
the thing follow not, nor come to pass, that is the thing which the LORD
hath not spoken" (v.22). The prophet spoke presumptuously. "Thou shalt not
be afraid of him" (v.22) — do not fear his threats or be intimidated by
his claims.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Moses speaks from the plains of Moab. The Levites "shall have no part nor inheritance with Israel" (v.1) — they are scattered throughout the land in 48 cities (cf. Numbers 35).

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Numbers     | 18      | 20-24              | Levites receive tithes instead of land.
v.2              | Psalm       | 16      | 5                  | ; 73:26 — "The LORD is the portion of mine inheritance."
v.2              | Lamentations | 3       | 24                 | "The LORD is my portion, says my soul."
v.4              | 1 Corinthians | 9       | 13-14              | Those who serve the altar live from the altar.
v.10             | Leviticus   | 18      | 21                 | ; 20:2-5 — Child sacrifice to Molech forbidden.
v.10             | 2 Kings     | 21      | 6                  | Manasseh practiced all these abominations.
v.10             | 1 Samuel    | 28      | 7-19               | Saul consults the medium at Endor.
v.11             | Isaiah      | 8       | 19                 | "Should not a people inquire of their God? Should they inquire of the dead on behalf of the living?"
v.12             | Leviticus   | 18      | 24-28              | The land vomits out nations for these sins.
v.13             | Genesis     | 17      | 1                  | "Walk before me, and be blameless."
v.15             | John        | 1       | 21                 | ,25; 6:14; 7:40 — "Are you that prophet?" / "This is of a truth that prophet."
v.15             | Acts        | 3       | 22-23              | Peter identifies Jesus as this Prophet.
v.15             | Acts        | 7       | 37                 | Stephen quotes this verse about Christ.
v.18             | John        | 12      | 49-50              | Jesus: "I have not spoken of myself; but the Father which sent me, he gave me a commandment, what I should say."
v.18             | John        | 7       | 16                 | "My doctrine is not mine, but his that sent me."
v.19             | Hebrews     | 2       | 3                  | "How shall we escape, if we neglect so great salvation?"
v.19             | John        | 12      | 48                 | "The word that I have spoken, the same shall judge him in the last day."
v.20             | Deuteronomy | 13      | 1-5                | False prophets who lead to other gods.
v.22             | Jeremiah    | 28      | 9                  | "When the word of the prophet shall come to pass, then shall the prophet be known."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-18/
  Guzik identifies the Prophet of v.15 as ultimately fulfilled in Christ,
  noting that while there were intermediate prophets (Joshua, Samuel,
  Elijah, etc.), none was "like Moses" until Jesus — who was a mediator,
  lawgiver, deliverer, and spoke face-to-face with God. He emphasizes that
  the occult prohibition is absolute: Israel's alternative to divination
  is not ignorance but divine revelation through the Prophet.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'I will raise them up a Prophet like unto thee.' Here is the great
  promise of Christ in the Old Testament. Moses was the mediator of the
  old covenant; Christ is the mediator of the new. Moses spoke God's words
  to the people; Christ IS the Word of God to the people. Moses delivered
  from Egypt; Christ delivers from sin. Moses was faithful in God's house
  as a servant; Christ is faithful as the Son over His own house
  (Hebrews 3:5-6). Every prophet pointed forward to this Prophet — the
  final, complete, perfect revelation of God to man."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "the offerings of the LORD made by fire, and his inheritance" / ESV: "the LORD's food offerings as their inheritance" — Hebrew "ishei YHWH v'nachalato." "Ishei" = fire offerings. The Levites eat from what is offered to God on the altar — God shares His table with His ministers.
v.3              | KJV         | "the maw" / ESV: "the stomach" — Hebrew "ha-qevah." The fourth stomach of a ruminant animal. KJV's archaic "maw" = stomach. These portions (shoulder, cheeks, stomach) were the priest's share from every sacrifice.
v.10             | KJV         | "observer of times" / ESV: "tells fortunes" — Hebrew "m'onen." Uncertain etymology. Possibly from "anan" (cloud) = reading clouds/weather signs, or from "ayin" (eye) = casting the evil eye. KJV and ESV interpret differently; both refer to forbidden divination practices.
v.10             | KJV         | "a witch" / ESV: "a sorcerer" — Hebrew "m'khashef." One who uses incantations, potions, or rituals to manipulate supernatural forces. KJV's "witch" carries later English connotations; ESV's "sorcerer" is more neutral. Same word as Exodus 22:18: "Thou shalt not suffer a witch to live."
v.11             | KJV         | "a consulter with familiar spirits" / ESV: "a medium" — Hebrew "sho'el ov." "Ov" = a spirit, possibly a pit-spirit or ancestral ghost. One who consults such spirits for information. KJV's "familiar spirits" = spirits supposedly familiar with the dead. ESV's "medium" = the modern equivalent term.
v.13             | KJV         | "Thou shalt be perfect with the LORD thy God" / ESV: "You shall be blameless before the LORD your God" — Hebrew "tamim tihyeh im YHWH elohekha." "Tamim" = complete, whole, blameless, without defect. Not sinless perfection but wholehearted devotion — undivided loyalty. Same word used of Noah (Genesis 6:9) and Abraham (Genesis 17:1).
v.15             | KJV         | "a Prophet from the midst of thee, of thy brethren, like unto me" / ESV: "a prophet like me from among you, from your brothers" — Hebrew "navi mi-qirb'kha me-acheicha kamoni." Three qualifications: (1) "from your midst" — from Israel; (2) "from your brothers" — an Israelite; (3) "like me" — like Moses in function and authority. Fulfilled in Christ alone.
v.18             | KJV         | "I will put my words in his mouth" / ESV: "I will put my words in his mouth" — Hebrew "v'natati d'varai b'fiv." God's words in the Prophet's mouth. This is the definition of prophetic inspiration: God's words spoken through a human vessel. Perfectly fulfilled in Christ (John 12:49).

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
The Prophet Like Moses | The Messianic figure promised in vv.15-18. Like Moses in being: (1) an Israelite, (2) a mediator between God and man, (3) a speaker of God's words, (4) a deliverer. Fulfilled in Jesus Christ (Acts 3:22; 7:37). The ultimate and final Prophet. | 
Divination        | Hebrew "qesem" — the practice of seeking hidden knowledge through occult means (casting lots, reading entrails, interpreting signs). Forbidden because it bypasses God's chosen means of revelation (the Prophet/the Word). | 
Necromancy        | Hebrew "doresh el ha-metim" — "one who inquires of the dead." Attempting to communicate with deceased persons for information or guidance. Forbidden absolutely. The dead cannot help the living (Isaiah 8:19). | 
Medium            | Hebrew "ov" — one who claims to channel spirits of the dead. The medium at Endor (1 Samuel 28) is the most famous biblical example. Saul's consultation of her was a covenant-breaking act that sealed his judgment. | 
Familiar Spirit   | KJV term for "ov" — a spirit supposedly familiar with or connected to the dead. The term implies an ongoing relationship between the medium and the spirit entity. | KJV term
Blameless/Perfect | Hebrew "tamim" — wholeness, completeness, undivided devotion. Not sinless perfection but wholehearted loyalty to God without mixture of occult practices. The opposite of divided allegiance between God and demonic sources. | 
Patrimony         | Hebrew "mimkarav al ha-avot" — property inherited from fathers. A Levite who sells family property and moves to the central sanctuary retains those proceeds in addition to his priestly portions. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "The LORD is their inheritance" (v.2). The Levites had no land because
   they had God Himself. This is the highest privilege, not a deprivation.
   Under the new covenant, every believer can say: "The LORD is my portion"
   (Lamentations 3:24). God Himself is better than any earthly inheritance.

2. The comprehensive occult prohibition (vv.10-11). Nine categories cover
   every possible form of supernatural consultation outside of God. This
   includes modern equivalents: horoscopes, tarot cards, psychics, séances,
   Ouija boards, and any attempt to access spiritual knowledge apart from
   God's word. The prohibition is absolute — not "be careful" but "there
   shall not be found among you."

3. "Because of these abominations the LORD thy God doth drive them out"
   (v.12). Occult practices are not harmless curiosity — they are the
   reason God judges nations. What destroyed the Canaanites will destroy
   anyone who practices these things. God's patience has limits.

4. The Prophet like Moses (vv.15-18). Israel's alternative to the occult is
   not ignorance but revelation. God does not leave His people without
   guidance — He sends His Prophet. In Christ, we have the final, complete
   word from God (Hebrews 1:1-2). We need no other source of spiritual
   knowledge.

5. "I will require it of him" (v.19). Rejecting Christ is not neutral — it
   brings God's judgment. The Prophet speaks God's words; refusing to
   listen is refusing God Himself. This is the most serious warning in
   Scripture: "How shall we escape, if we neglect so great salvation?"
   (Hebrews 2:3).

6. Two tests of a prophet: (1) Does his message lead to God? (chapter 13);
   (2) Does his prediction come true? (v.22). Both must be satisfied. A
   prophet whose signs come true but whose message leads astray is false
   (13:1-3). A prophet whose message sounds right but whose predictions
   fail is also false (18:22). Truth and fulfillment together.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Deuteronomy Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/deuteronomy/

David Guzik — Deuteronomy 18 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-18/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

19.ESV:
1. “When the LORD your God cuts off the nations whose land the LORD your God is giving you, and you dispossess them and dwell in their cities and in their houses,
2. you shall set apart three cities for yourselves in the land that the LORD your God is giving you to possess.
3. You shall measure the distances and divide into three parts the area of the land that the LORD your God gives you as a possession, so that any manslayer can flee to them.
4. “This is the provision for the manslayer, who by fleeing there may save his life. If anyone kills his neighbor unintentionally without having hated him in the past—
5. as when someone goes into the forest with his neighbor to cut wood, and his hand swings the axe to cut down a tree, and the head slips from the handle and strikes his neighbor so that he dies—he may flee to one of these cities and live,
6. lest the avenger of blood in hot anger pursue the manslayer and overtake him, because the way is long, and strike him fatally, though the man did not deserve to die, since he had not hated his neighbor in the past.
7. Therefore I command you, You shall set apart three cities.
8. And if the LORD your God enlarges your territory, as he has sworn to your fathers, and gives you all the land that he promised to give to your fathers—
9. provided you are careful to keep all this commandment, which I command you today, by loving the LORD your God and by walking ever in his ways—then you shall add three other cities to these three,
10. lest innocent blood be shed in your land that the LORD your God is giving you for an inheritance, and so the guilt of bloodshed be upon you.
11. “But if anyone hates his neighbor and lies in wait for him and attacks him and strikes him fatally so that he dies, and he flees into one of these cities,
12. then the elders of his city shall send and take him from there, and hand him over to the avenger of blood, so that he may die.
13. Your eye shall not pity him, but you shall purge the guilt of innocent blood from Israel, so that it may be well with you.
14. “You shall not move your neighbor’s landmark, which the men of old have set, in the inheritance that you will hold in the land that the LORD your God is giving you to possess.
15. “A single witness shall not suffice against a person for any crime or for any wrong in connection with any offense that he has committed. Only on the evidence of two witnesses or of three witnesses shall a charge be established.
16. If a malicious witness arises to accuse a person of wrongdoing,
17. then both parties to the dispute shall appear before the LORD, before the priests and the judges who are in office in those days.
18. The judges shall inquire diligently, and if the witness is a false witness and has accused his brother falsely,
19. then you shall do to him as he had meant to do to his brother. So you shall purge the evil from your midst.
20. And the rest shall hear and fear, and shall never again commit any such evil among you.
21. Your eye shall not pity. It shall be life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.

1. When the LORD thy God hath cut off the nations, whose land the LORD thy God giveth thee, and thou succeedest them, and dwellest in their cities, and in their houses;

3. Thou shalt prepare thee a way, and divide the coasts of thy land, which the LORD thy God giveth thee to inherit, into three parts, that every slayer may flee thither.
4. And this is the case of the slayer, which shall flee thither, that he may live: Whoso killeth his neighbour ignorantly, whom he hated not in time past;

6. Lest the avenger of the blood pursue the slayer, while his heart is hot, and overtake him, because the way is long, and slay him; whereas he was not worthy of death, inasmuch as he hated him not in time past.

8. And if the LORD thy God enlarge thy coast, as he hath sworn unto thy fathers, and give thee all the land which he promised to give unto thy fathers;

14. Thou shalt not remove thy neighbour’s landmark, which they of old time have set in thine inheritance, which thou shalt inherit in the land that the LORD thy God giveth thee to possess it.
15. One witness shall not rise up against a man for any iniquity, or for any sin, in any sin that he sinneth: at the mouth of two witnesses, or at the mouth of three witnesses, shall the matter be established.

20. And those which remain shall hear, and fear, and shall henceforth commit no more any such evil among you.
21. And thine eye shall not pity; but life shall go for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.

19.NET:
1. When the Lord your God destroys the nations whose land he is about to give you and you dispossess them and settle in their cities and houses,
2. you must set apart for yourselves three cities in the middle of your land that the Lord your God is giving you as a possession.
3. You shall build a roadway and divide into thirds the whole extent of your land that the Lord your God is providing as your inheritance; anyone who kills another person should flee to the closest of these cities.
4. Now this is the law pertaining to one who flees there in order to live, if he has accidentally killed another without hating him at the time of the accident.
5. Suppose he goes with someone else to the forest to cut wood and when he raises the ax to cut the tree, the ax head flies loose from the handle and strikes his fellow worker so hard that he dies. The person responsible may then flee to one of these cities to save himself.
6. Otherwise the blood avenger will chase after the killer in the heat of his anger, eventually overtake him, and kill him, though this is not a capital case since he did not hate him at the time of the accident.
7. Therefore, I am commanding you to set apart for yourselves three cities.
8. If the Lord your God enlarges your borders as he promised your ancestors and gives you all the land he pledged to them,
9. and then you are careful to observe all these commandments I am giving you today (namely, to love the Lord your God and to always walk in his ways), then you must add three more cities to these three.
10. You must not shed innocent blood in your land that the Lord your God is giving you as an inheritance, for that would make you guilty.
11. However, suppose a person hates someone else and stalks him, attacks him, kills him, and then flees to one of these cities.
12. The elders of his own city must send for him and remove him from there to deliver him over to the blood avenger to die.
13. You must not pity him, but purge from Israel the guilt of shedding innocent blood, so that it may go well with you.
14. You must not encroach on your neighbor’s property, which will have been defined in the inheritance you will obtain in the land the Lord your God is giving you.
15. A single witness may not testify against another person for any trespass or sin that he commits. A matter may be legally established only on the testimony of two or three witnesses.
16. If a false witness testifies against another person and accuses him of a crime,
17. then both parties to the controversy must stand before the Lord, that is, before the priests and judges who will be in office in those days.
18. The judges will thoroughly investigate the matter, and if the witness should prove to be false and to have given false testimony against the accused,
19. you must do to him what he had intended to do to the accused. In this way you will purge the evil from among you.
20. The rest of the people will hear and become afraid to keep doing such evil among you.
21. You must not show pity; the principle will be a life for a life, an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth, a hand for a hand, and a foot for a foot.

19.notes:
Deuteronomy - Chapter 19
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God establishes cities of refuge for the manslayer and laws requiring multiple witnesses — justice must be careful, fair, and thorough.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Deuteronomy is the fifth and final book of the Pentateuch,
written at the end of the 40 years of wilderness wandering (approximately
1406 BC). Hebrew title: "Devarim" — "Words/Things." Greek title:
"Deuteronomion" — "Second Law" (a repetition/expansion of the Law for the
new generation). The book consists of Moses' farewell speeches to Israel on
the plains of Moab, just before they enter the Promised Land. Moses will
not enter with them — these are his final words to the nation he has led
for 40 years.

Historical Context: Chapter 19 addresses the protection of life and the
integrity of justice through three related laws: (1) cities of refuge for
accidental manslayers (vv.1-13), (2) the prohibition against moving
boundary markers (v.14), and (3) the law of witnesses and false testimony
(vv.15-21). The cities of refuge were first established in Numbers 35:9-34
and are expanded here for life in the Promised Land. Three cities are to be
set apart in the land west of the Jordan (in addition to the three already
designated east of the Jordan — Deuteronomy 4:41-43), with provision for
three more if God enlarges the territory (vv.8-9). These cities
distinguished between intentional murder and accidental killing — a
distinction fundamental to justice. The accidental killer could flee to a
city of refuge and be safe from the "avenger of blood" (the victim's
kinsman). The intentional murderer received no such protection (vv.11-13).
The boundary marker law (v.14) protected property rights — moving a
landmark was theft of inheritance. The witness laws (vv.15-21) reinforce
the two-witness requirement (from 17:6) and add the crucial principle of
lex talionis for false witnesses: a false accuser receives the punishment
he sought for his victim (v.19). This is justice in its purest form — the
punishment fits the crime exactly.

Cities of Refuge — Protection for Accidental Killers (vv.1-10): "Thou
shalt separate three cities for thee in the midst of thy land" (v.2).
"Thou shalt prepare thee a way" (v.3) — roads must be maintained to the
cities so the manslayer can reach them quickly. The land divided into three
parts so a city is always accessible (v.3). The qualifying case: "Whoso
killeth his neighbour ignorantly, whom he hated not in time past" (v.4).
Example: a man cutting wood, the axe head flies off and kills his neighbor
(v.5). He flees to the city and lives (v.5). Purpose: to prevent the
avenger of blood from killing him "while his heart is hot" (v.6) — before
justice can be determined. The man "was not worthy of death, inasmuch as
he hated him not in time past" (v.6). If God enlarges the territory, three
more cities are added (vv.8-9) — conditional on obedience (v.9). Purpose:
"That innocent blood be not shed in thy land... and so blood be upon thee"
(v.10). Failing to provide refuge makes the nation guilty of innocent
blood.

No Refuge for Murderers (vv.11-13): "But if any man hate his neighbour,
and lie in wait for him, and rise up against him, and smite him mortally"
(v.11). Premeditated murder — hatred, ambush, lethal attack. If he flees
to a city of refuge: "the elders of his city shall send and fetch him
thence, and deliver him into the hand of the avenger of blood, that he may
die" (v.12). The city of refuge does not protect murderers. "Thine eye
shall not pity him" (v.13). "Thou shalt put away the guilt of innocent
blood from Israel, that it may go well with thee" (v.13). Unpunished
murder pollutes the land.

Boundary Markers (v.14): "Thou shalt not remove thy neighbour's landmark,
which they of old time have set in thine inheritance" (v.14). Boundary
stones marked property lines. Moving them was theft — stealing land from
your neighbor by stealth. The inheritance was God-given; to move a
landmark was to steal what God had assigned.

The Two-Witness Rule (v.15): "One witness shall not rise up against a man
for any iniquity, or for any sin" (v.15). No conviction on a single
testimony — for ANY offense. "At the mouth of two witnesses, or at the
mouth of three witnesses, shall the matter be established" (v.15).

The Law of False Witnesses (vv.16-21): "If a false witness rise up against
any man to testify against him that which is wrong" (v.16). Both parties
appear "before the LORD, before the priests and the judges" (v.17). "The
judges shall make diligent inquisition" (v.18) — thorough investigation.
If the witness is false: "Then shall ye do unto him, as he had thought to
have done unto his brother" (v.19). The false witness receives the
punishment he sought for his victim. "So shalt thou put the evil away from
among you" (v.19). Deterrence: "those which remain shall hear, and fear"
(v.20). The principle of proportional justice: "life shall go for life,
eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot" (v.21).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Moses speaks from the plains of Moab. Three cities of refuge to be set in the land west of Jordan (v.2), with potential for three more if borders expand (v.8). Roads must be built to ensure access (v.3).

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Numbers     | 35      | 9-34               | The original cities of refuge legislation.
v.2              | Deuteronomy | 4       | 41-43              | Three cities east of the Jordan already designated.
v.2              | Joshua      | 20      | 1-9                | The cities of refuge established in the land.
v.5              | Numbers     | 35      | 22-25              | Accidental killing defined.
v.6              | Numbers     | 35      | 12                 | ,19 — The avenger of blood (go'el ha-dam).
v.10             | Numbers     | 35      | 33-34              | Blood pollutes the land; only blood atones.
v.11             | Numbers     | 35      | 16-21              | Premeditated murder defined.
v.13             | Genesis     | 9       | 6                  | "Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed."
v.14             | Proverbs    | 22      | 28                 | "Remove not the ancient landmark, which your fathers have set."
v.14             | Proverbs    | 23      | 10                 | "Remove not the old landmark; and enter not into the fields of the fatherless."
v.14             | Hosea       | 5       | 10                 | "The princes of Judah were like them that remove the bound."
v.15             | Deuteronomy | 17      | 6                  | Two witnesses for capital cases.
v.15             | Matthew     | 18      | 16                 | Jesus applies the two-witness rule.
v.15             | 1 Timothy   | 5       | 19                 | "Against an elder receive not an accusation, but before two or three witnesses."
v.19             | Proverbs    | 19      | 5                  | ,9 — "A false witness shall not be unpunished."
v.21             | Exodus      | 21      | 23-25              | The original lex talionis.
v.21             | Leviticus   | 24      | 19-20              | "As he has done, so shall it be done to him."
v.21             | Matthew     | 5       | 38-39              | Jesus addresses the lex talionis in personal relationships.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-19/
  Guzik notes that the cities of refuge are a picture of Christ — the
  sinner flees to Him for protection from the just penalty of the law.
  He emphasizes that the roads to the cities had to be maintained and
  clearly marked — access to refuge must be easy. He highlights the false
  witness law as one of the most just principles in Scripture: you receive
  what you intended for another.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "The cities of refuge are a picture of Christ. The manslayer fled for
  his life — and so does the sinner. The roads were kept clear and the
  gates always open — and so is the way to Christ. No one was turned away
  who came — and no one is turned away who comes to Christ. But mark this:
  the murderer found no refuge there. The city protected the innocent, not
  the guilty. Christ saves sinners who flee to Him — but those who
  presumptuously continue in sin find no shelter in His name."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "Thou shalt prepare thee a way" / ESV: "You shall measure the distances" — Hebrew "takhin l'kha ha-derekh." "Takhin" = to prepare, establish. KJV emphasizes road preparation; ESV emphasizes distance measurement. The Talmud records that roads to cities of refuge were maintained, widened, and marked with signs.
v.4              | KJV         | "killeth his neighbour ignorantly" / ESV: "kills his neighbor unintentionally" — Hebrew "yakkeh et re'ehu bivli da'at." "Bivli da'at" = without knowledge/intent. The key distinction: no prior hatred, no intent to harm. Accidental, not premeditated.
v.5              | KJV         | "the head slippeth from the helve" / ESV: "the head slips from the handle" — Hebrew "v'nashal ha-barzel min ha-etz." "Barzel" = iron (the axe head). "Etz" = wood (the handle). KJV's "helve" is an archaic word for axe handle.
v.6              | KJV         | "the avenger of the blood" / ESV: "the avenger of blood" — Hebrew "go'el ha-dam." "Go'el" = kinsman-redeemer, the one who restores/avenges for the family. The same word used of Boaz (Ruth) and of God as Israel's Redeemer. The go'el ha-dam had the right/ duty to avenge a kinsman's death.
v.14             | KJV         | "thy neighbour's landmark" / ESV: "your neighbor's landmark" — Hebrew "g'vul re'akha." "G'vul" = boundary, border. Boundary stones were the ancient equivalent of property deeds. Moving them was a form of theft that was difficult to detect and prove.
v.19             | KJV         | "as he had thought to have done unto his brother" / ESV: "as he had meant to do to his brother" — Hebrew "ka'asher zamam la'asot l'achiv." "Zamam" = to plan, devise, intend. The punishment is based on INTENT — what the false witness planned to accomplish through his lie. Even if the plot failed, the false witness receives the intended punishment.
v.21             | KJV         | "life shall go for life, eye for eye" / ESV: "It shall be life for life, eye for eye" — Hebrew "nefesh b'nefesh, ayin b'ayin." The lex talionis (law of retaliation). This is not vengeance but proportionality — the punishment must match the crime exactly. It limits punishment as much as it requires it: no MORE than an eye for an eye.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Cities of Refuge  | Designated cities where accidental killers could flee for protection from the avenger of blood. Three west of Jordan, three east (with three more if territory expanded). They distinguished between murder and manslaughter, protecting the innocent while ensuring justice for the guilty. | 
Avenger of Blood  | Hebrew "go'el ha-dam" — the kinsman responsible for avenging a murdered relative. Had the legal right to kill the murderer. The cities of refuge prevented him from killing an accidental manslayer before justice could be determined. | 
Landmark/Boundary | Hebrew "g'vul" — a stone marker indicating property boundaries. Moving it was theft of inheritance. Protected by law because land was God's gift to each family — to steal land was to steal God's provision. | 
Lex Talionis      | Latin: "law of retaliation." The principle of proportional justice: the punishment matches the crime exactly. "Eye for eye, tooth for tooth." This LIMITS punishment (no excessive revenge) as much as it requires it (no inadequate justice). | 
False Witness     | One who testifies falsely against another in court. Under Israelite law, the false witness receives the punishment he sought for his victim. This made false accusation extremely dangerous — you risked your own life by lying. | 
Put the Evil Away | Hebrew "bi'arta ha-ra" — the recurring Deuteronomic phrase for removing wickedness from the community. The community is responsible for purging evil to maintain covenant purity and God's blessing. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Cities of refuge — a picture of Christ. The accidental killer fled for
   his life to a designated place of safety. The sinner flees to Christ for
   refuge from the just penalty of the law. The roads were maintained and
   clear — the way to Christ is open and accessible. "We have fled for
   refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us" (Hebrews 6:18).

2. Intent matters (vv.4-6,11). God distinguishes between accidental harm
   and premeditated evil. The same act (killing) receives different
   treatment based on the heart behind it. God judges the heart, not merely
   the outcome. This is true justice.

3. "Thou shalt not remove thy neighbour's landmark" (v.14). Stealing by
   stealth — gradually encroaching on what belongs to another — is
   condemned. This applies to property, reputation, relationships, and
   rights. Incremental theft is still theft.

4. The false witness receives his own intended punishment (v.19). This is
   perfect justice and a powerful deterrent. Before you accuse someone,
   consider: are you willing to receive the punishment you seek for them?
   If not, your accusation may be motivated by malice rather than truth.

5. "Eye for eye" (v.21) is not vengeance but proportionality. It LIMITS
   punishment as much as it requires it. You may not take a life for an
   eye, or a hand for a tooth. Justice must be proportional — neither
   excessive nor lenient. This is the foundation of just sentencing.

6. "Those which remain shall hear, and fear" (v.20). Public justice serves
   a deterrent function. When false witnesses are punished, others think
   twice before lying. When justice is visible, wickedness is restrained.
   Hidden or delayed justice emboldens evil.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Deuteronomy Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/deuteronomy/

David Guzik — Deuteronomy 19 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/deuteronomy-19/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

20.ESV:
1. “When you go out to war against your enemies, and see horses and chariots and an army larger than your own, you shall not be afraid of them, for the LORD your God is with you, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt.
2. And when you draw near to the battle, the priest shall come forward and speak to the people
3. and shall say to them, ‘Hear, O Israel, today you are drawing near for battle against your enemies: let not your heart faint. Do not fear or panic or be in dread of them,
4. for the LORD your God is he who goes with you to fight for you against your enemies, to give you the victory.’
5. Then the officers shall speak to the people, saying, ‘Is there any man who has built a new house and has not dedicated it? Let him go back to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man dedicate it.
6. And is there any man who has planted a vineyard and has not enjoyed its fruit? Let him go back to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man enjoy its fruit.
7. And is there any man who has betrothed a wife and has not taken her? Let him go back to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man take her.’
8. And the officers shall speak further to the people, and say, ‘Is there any man who is fearful and fainthearted? Let him go back to his house, lest he make the heart of his fellows melt like his own.’
9. And when the officers have finished speaking to the people, then commanders shall be appointed at the head of the people.
10. “When you draw near to a city to fight against it, offer terms of peace to it.
11. And if it responds to you peaceably and it opens to you, then all the people who are found in it shall do forced labor for you and shall serve you.
12. But if it makes no peace with you, but makes war against you, then you shall besiege it.
13. And when the LORD your God gives it into your hand, you shall put all its males to the sword,
14. but the women and the little ones, the livestock, and everything else in the city, all its spoil, you shall take as plunder for yourselves. And you shall enjoy the spoil of your enemies, which the LORD your God has given you.
15. Thus you shall do to all the cities that are very far from you, which are not cities of the nations here.
16. But in the cities of these peoples that the LORD your God is giving you for an inheritance, you shall save alive nothing that breathes,
17. but you shall devote them to complete destruction, the Hittites and the Amorites, the Canaanites and the Perizzites, the Hivites and the Jebusites, as the LORD your God has commanded,
18. that they may not teach you to do according to all their abominable practices that they have done for their gods, and so you sin against the LORD your God.
19. “When you besiege a city for a long time, making war against it in order to take it, you shall not destroy its trees by wielding an axe against them. You may eat from them, but you shall not cut them down. Are the trees in the field human, that they should be besieged by you?
20. Only the trees that you know are not trees for food you may destroy and cut down, that you may build siegeworks against the city that makes war with you, until it falls.
20.KJV:

2. And it shall be, when ye are come nigh unto the battle, that the priest shall approach and speak unto the people,
3. And shall say unto them, Hear, O Israel, ye approach this day unto battle against your enemies: let not your hearts faint, fear not, and do not tremble, neither be ye terrified because of them;
4. For the LORD your God is he that goeth with you, to fight for you against your enemies, to save you.
5. And the officers shall speak unto the people, saying, What man is there that hath built a new house, and hath not dedicated it? let him go and return to his house, lest he die in the battle, and another man dedicate it.
6. And what man is he that hath planted a vineyard, and hath not yet eaten of it? let him also go and return unto his house, lest he die in the battle, and another man eat of it.
7. And what man is there that hath betrothed a wife, and hath not taken her? let him go and return unto his house, lest he die in the battle, and another man take her.
8. And the officers shall speak further unto the people, and they shall say, What man is there that is fearful and fainthearted? let him go and return unto his house, lest his brethren’s heart faint as well as his heart.

20. Only the trees which thou knowest that they be not trees for meat, thou shalt destroy and cut them down; and thou shalt build bulwarks against the city that maketh war with thee, until it be subdued.

2. And it shall be, when ye draw nigh unto the battle, that the priest shall approach and speak unto the people,

19. When thou shalt besiege a city a long time, in making war against it to take it, thou shalt not destroy the trees thereof by wielding an axe against them; for thou mayest eat of them, and thou shalt not cut them down; for is the tree of the field man, that it should be besieged of thee?
20. Only the trees of which thou knowest that they are not trees for food, thou shalt destroy and cut them down; and thou shalt build bulwarks against the city that maketh war with thee, until it fall.
20.NET:
1. When you go to war against your enemies and see chariotry and troops who outnumber you, do not be afraid of them, for the Lord your God, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt, is with you.
2. As you move forward for battle, the priest will approach and say to the soldiers,
3. “Listen, Israel! Today you are moving forward to do battle with your enemies. Do not be fainthearted. Do not fear and tremble or be terrified because of them,
4. for the Lord your God goes with you to fight on your behalf against your enemies to give you victory.”
5. Moreover, the officers are to say to the troops, “Who among you has built a new house and not dedicated it? He may go home, lest he die in battle and someone else dedicate it.
6. Or who among you has planted a vineyard and not benefited from it? He may go home, lest he die in battle and someone else benefit from it.
7. Or who among you has become engaged to a woman but has not married her? He may go home, lest he die in battle and someone else marry her.”
8. In addition, the officers are to say to the troops, “Who among you is afraid and fainthearted? He may go home so that he will not make his fellow soldier’s heart as fearful as his own.”
9. Then, when the officers have finished speaking, they must appoint unit commanders to lead the troops.
10. When you approach a city to wage war against it, offer it terms of peace.
11. If it accepts your terms and submits to you, all the people found in it will become your slaves.
12. If it does not accept terms of peace but makes war with you, then you are to lay siege to it.
13. The Lord your God will deliver it over to you, and you must kill every single male by the sword.
14. However, the women, little children, cattle, and anything else in the city—all its plunder—you may take for yourselves as spoil. You may take from your enemies the plunder that the Lord your God has given you.
15. This is how you are to deal with all those cities located far from you, those that do not belong to these nearby nations.
16. As for the cities of these peoples that the Lord your God is going to give you as an inheritance, you must not allow a single living thing to survive.
17. Instead you must utterly annihilate them—the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites—just as the Lord your God has commanded you,
18. so that they cannot teach you all the abhorrent ways they worship their gods, causing you to sin against the Lord your God.
19. If you besiege a city for a long time while attempting to capture it, you must not chop down its trees, for you may eat fruit from them and should not cut them down. A tree in the field is not human that you should besiege it!
20. However, you may chop down any tree you know is not suitable for food, and you may use it to build siege works against the city that is making war with you until that city falls.
